The commencement dates are determined by a table that lists specific sections, parts, and divisions along with their effective dates, which vary from the day the Act receives Royal Assent to 12 months after the end of a specified period.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of key terms for financial and data contexts
sections.summary: This section defines specific terms used in the context of financial services and data management. It establishes meanings for terms such as 'Comptroller-General of Customs', 'constitutional corporation', 'contribution', 'control', 'controller of an eligible gaming machine venue', and 'correspondent banking relationship'. Each definition provides precise criteria for these terms, often referencing other legislation or acts to ensure clarity and consistency in their application across financial and regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are foundational for understanding data transfer contexts, particularly for terms like 'correspondent banking relationship' which may impact cross-border financial data flows. They establish the scope of terms relevant to international data transfers and financial services.

requirements.text: Comptroller-General of Customs means the person who is the Comptroller-General of Customs in accordance with subsection 11(3) or 14(2) of the Australian Border Force Act 2015.
constitutional corporation means a corporation to which paragraph 51(xx) of the Constitution applies.
contribution, in relation to an RSA, has the same meaning as in the Retirement Savings Accounts Act 1997.
control has the meaning given by section 11.
controller of an eligible gaming machine venue has the meaning given by section 13.

correspondent banking relationship means a relationship that involves the provision by a financial institution (the first financial institution) of banking services to another financial institution, where: (a) the first financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in a particular country; (b) the other financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the other financial institution in another country; (c) the correspondent banking relationship relates, in whole or in part, to those permanent establishments; (d) the relationship is not of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules; (e) the banking services are not of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules.
For this purpose, banking service includes anything that, under the AML/CTF Rules, is taken to be a banking service for the purposes of this definition.

sections.source_quote: Comptroller-General of Customs means the person who is the Comptroller-General of Customs in accordance with subsection 11(3) or 14(2) of the Australian Border Force Act 2015.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of financial and data-related terms
sections.summary: This section provides definitions for terms critical to financial services and data management, including 'customer', 'customs officer', 'damage', 'data', 'data storage device', 'debit card', 'debit card account', 'deductible gift recipient', 'de facto partner', 'Defence Department', 'Defence Minister', 'departing Australia superannuation payment', 'derivative', 'designated infringement notice provision', 'designated service', 'director of a company', 'Director-General of National Intelligence', 'disclose', 'disposing of', 'domestic politically exposed person', and others. These definitions clarify the scope and application of terms in financial and regulatory contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are essential for understanding data transfer contexts, particularly for terms like 'data' and 'customer' which directly impact how personal and financial data is handled across borders.

requirements.text: customer has the meaning given by section 6, and includes a prospective customer.
customs officer means an officer of Customs within the meaning of the Customs Act 1901.
damage, in relation to data, includes damage by erasure of data or addition of other data.
data includes: (a) information in any form; or (b) any program (or part of a program).
data storage device means a thing containing, or designed to contain, data for use by a computer.

debit card means: (a) a thing (whether real or virtual) that is intended for use by a person in obtaining access to an account that is held by the person for the purpose of withdrawing or depositing physical currency or obtaining goods or services; or (b) a thing (whether real or virtual) that may be used as a thing referred to in paragraph (a).
debit card account: if a debit card enables the holder of an account to debit the account, the account is a debit card account.
deductible gift recipient has the same meaning as in the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997.
de facto partner has the meaning given by the Acts Interpretation Act 1901.
Defence Department means the Department administered by the Defence Minister.
Defence Minister means the Minister responsible for administering the Defence Act 1903.
departing Australia superannuation payment has the same meaning as in the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997.
derivative: see subsections 7A(3) and (4).
designated infringement notice provision has the meaning given by subsection 184(4).
designated service has the meaning given by section 6.
director of a company includes a member of a body corporate incorporated for a public purpose by a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory.
Director-General of National Intelligence means the Director-General of National Intelligence holding office under the Office of National Intelligence Act 2018.
disclose means divulge or communicate.
disposing of: in determining whether something is a designated service, disposing of includes anything that, under the regulations, is taken to be disposing of for the purposes of this definition.

domestic politically exposed person means: (a) an individual who holds an office or position (whether or not in or for the Commonwealth) specified in the AML/CTF Rules; or (b) an individual who is a member of the legislature of the Commonwealth or of a State or Territory; or (c) a family member of an individual covered by paragraph (a) or (b); or (d) an individual who is known (having regard to information paragraph (a) or (b); or

sections.source_quote: customer has the meaning given by section 6, and includes a prospective customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of financial and data terms for international operations
sections.summary: This section defines terms relevant to international financial operations and data management, including 'eligible gaming machine venue', 'eligible international emissions unit', 'eligible place', 'embarkation area', 'engage in conduct', 'enrolment details', 'entrusted investigating official', 'evidential burden', 'examinee', 'examiner', 'examiner of the Australian Crime Commission', 'exchange settlement account', 'exempt financial market operator issue of a security or derivative', 'express trust', 'extension notice', 'external auditor', and 'factoring'. These definitions provide clarity for cross-border financial activities and data handling.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are critical for understanding international data transfers, particularly for terms like 'eligible place' and 'exchange settlement account' which may impact data storage and processing across borders.

requirements.text: eligible gaming machine venue has the meaning given by section 13.
eligible international emissions unit has the same meaning as in the Australian National Registry of Emissions Units Act 2.011.

eligible place means: (b) a warehouse in respect of which a warehouse licence (within the meaning of Part V of the Customs Act 1901) is in force; or (c) a port, airport, wharf or boarding station appointed under section 15 of the Customs Act 1901.
embarkation area means a section 234AA place within the meaning of the Customs Act 1901.
engage in conduct means: (a) do an act; or (b) omit to perform an act.
enrolment details, in relation to a person, means such information relating to the person as is specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

entrusted investigating official means: (a) the Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police; or (b) the Chief Executive Officer of the Australian Crime Commission; or (c) the Commissioner of Taxation; or (d) the Comptroller-General of Customs; or (e) the National Anti-Corruption Commissioner; or (f) an investigating officer.
evidential burden, in relation to a matter, means the burden of adducing or pointing to evidence that suggests a reasonable possibility that the matter exists or does not exist.
examinee, in relation to an examination under Division 3 of Part 14, means the person who appears for examination.

examiner, in relation to an examination under Division 3 of Part 14, means the AUSTRAC CEO and includes: (a) a delegate of the AUSTRAC CEO; and (b) a consultant engaged under subsection 225(1) to perform services as an examiner.
examiner of the Australian Crime Commission means an examiner within the meaning of the Australian Crime Commission Act 2002.
exchange settlement account means an account provided by the Reserve Bank of Australia that is used for the final settlement of obligations between holders of such accounts.

exempt financial market operator issue of a security or derivative means the making available of the security or derivative, by the operator of a financial market (within the meaning of Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001).
express trust includes formed. This definition does not apply to the expression unincorporated.
extension notice: see subsection 39B(1).
external auditor means a person who is qualified to perform audits under the relevant laws.
factoring includes anything that, under the regulations, is taken to be a guarantee for the purposes of this Act.

sections.source_quote: eligible gaming machine venue has the meaning given by section 13.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of government and international entities
sections.summary: This section defines terms related to government structures and international entities, including 'government body', 'guarantee', 'IGIS official', 'information obtained', 'infringement notice', 'infringement notice provision', 'institution', 'intermediary institution', 'international organisation politically exposed person', 'international value transfer service', 'investigating officer', and 'involves'. These definitions are crucial for understanding the scope of government and international operations in financial contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are essential for understanding international data transfers, particularly for terms like 'government body' and 'investigating officer' which may impact cross-border data handling and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: government body means: (a) the government of a country; or (b) an agency or authority of the government of a country; or (c) the government of part of a country; or (d) an agency or authority of the government of part of a country.
guarantee includes anything that, under the regulations, is taken to be a guarantee for the purposes of this Act.

IGIS official (short for Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security official) means: (a) the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security; or (b) any other person covered by subsection 32(1) of the incorporated includes formed.
information obtained includes information obtained as a result of the production of a document.
infringement notice means an infringement notice under section 184.
infringement notice provision has the meaning given by subsection 184(1A).
institution: see subsection 63A(12).
intermediary institution: see subsections 63A(9) and (10).

international organisation politically exposed person means: (a) an individual who is entrusted with a prominent public function, position or office of a public international organisation, including a head, deputy head or board member in a public international organisation; (b) a family member of an individual covered by (a); (c) an individual who is known (having regard to information paragraph (a); or
international value transfer service has the meaning given by section 45.

investigating officer means: (a) a taxation officer; or (b) an AFP member; or (c) a customs officer (other than the Comptroller-General of Customs); or (d) an examiner of the Australian Crime Commission; or (e) a member of the staff of the Australian Crime Commission; or (f) a National Anti-Corruption Commission officer.
involves includes relates to.
issue, when used in relation to a security or derivative, has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001.

sections.source_quote: government body means: (a) the government of a country; or (b) an agency or authority of the government of a country; or (c) the government of part of a country; or (d) an agency or authority of the government of part of a country.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of KYC information for reporting entities
sections.summary: This section defines KYC information as data that provides reasonable grounds for a reporting entity to establish matters under subsection 28(2) or enables identifying or assessing a customer's ML/TF risk. The definition specifies the types of information that qualify as KYC information for reporting entities under the Act.

requirements.text: KYC information about a customer of a reporting entity means information about the customer that: (a) provides reasonable grounds for the reporting entity to establish the matters mentioned in subsection 28(2); or (b) enables the reporting entity to identify or assess the ML/TF risk of the customer.

sections.source_quote: 27 Compilation KYC information (short for know your customer information) about a customer of a reporting entity means information about the customer that: (a) provides reasonable grounds for the reporting entity to establish the matters mentioned in subsection 28(2); or (b) enables the reporting entity to identify or assess the ML/TF risk of the customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of ML/TF risk for reporting entities
sections.summary: This section defines ML/TF risk as the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that a reporting entity may reasonably face when providing designated services to a customer. It also specifies that a reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment includes the assessment as updated under section 26D.

requirements.text: ML/TF risk, of a customer, means the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that a reporting entity may reasonably face in providing its designated service, or designated services, to the customer.

ML/TF risk assessment of a reporting entity: (a) means the risk assessment undertaken by the reporting entity under section 26C; and (b) if the assessment is updated under section 26D—includes the risk assessment as updated.

sections.source_quote: 29 Compilation ML/TF risk, of a customer, means the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that a reporting entity may reasonably face in providing its designated service, or designated services, to the customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of nested services relationship
sections.summary: This section defines a nested services relationship as a relationship where a reporting entity provides a designated service to a customer that is also a remitter, virtual asset service provider or financial institution, with the customer providing services to their own customers in another country, not involving correspondent banking relationships.

requirements.text: nested services relationship means a relationship that involves the provision of a designated service by a reporting entity that is a remitter, virtual asset service provider or financial institution to a customer that is a remitter, virtual asset service provider or financial institution where: (a) the reporting entity provides the designated service at or through a permanent establishment in one country; (b) the customer uses the designated service to provide services to its own customers at or through a permanent establishment in another country; and (c) the relationship is not a correspondent banking relationship.

sections.source_quote: 31 Compilation nested services relationship means a relationship that involves the provision of a designated service by a reporting entity that is a remitter, virtual asset service provider or financial institution to a customer that is a remitter, virtual asset service provider or financial institution where: (a) the reporting entity provides the designated service at or through a permanent establishment in one country; (b) the customer uses the designated service to provide services to its own customers at or through a permanent establishment in another country; and (c) the relationship is not a correspondent banking relationship.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of politically exposed person
sections.summary: This section defines a politically exposed person as a domestic politically exposed person, a foreign politically exposed person, or an international organisation politically exposed person. The definition is used to identify individuals who may be subject to enhanced due diligence requirements under the Act.

requirements.text: politically exposed person means: (a) a domestic politically exposed person; or (b) a foreign politically exposed person; or (c) an international organisation politically exposed person.

sections.source_quote: 33 Compilation politically exposed person means: (a) a domestic politically exposed person; or (b) a foreign politically exposed person; or (c) an international organisation politically exposed person.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of proliferation financing
sections.summary: This section defines proliferation financing as conduct that amounts to an offence against the Charter of the United Nations Act 1945, Autonomous Sanctions Act 2

requirements.text: proliferation financing means conduct that amounts to: (a) an offence against the Charter of the United Nations Act 1945, or regulations made under that Act, that is prescribed by regulations made under this Act for the purposes of this paragraph; or (b) an offence against the Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011, or a contravention of regulations made under that Act, that involves sanctions addressing the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction; or (c) an offence against the Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011, or a contravention of regulations made under that Act, that is prescribed by regulations made under this Act for the purposes of this paragraph; or (d) the provision of assets (including funds) or financial services, or other dealing with assets, in contravention of a law of the Commonwealth that implements an international agreement, convention or treaty relating to the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction; or (e) an offence against a law of a State or Territory that corresponds to an offence referred to in paragraph (a), (b), (c) or (d); or (f) an offence against a law of a foreign country or a part of a paragraph (a), (b), (c), (d) or (e); or (g) an offence against a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory that is prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph.

sections.source_quote: 35 Compilation proliferation financing means conduct that amounts to: (a) an offence against the Charter of the United Nations Act 1945, or regulations made under that Act, that is prescribed by regulations made under this Act for the purposes of this paragraph; or (b) an offence against the Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011, or a contravention of regulations made under that Act, that involves sanctions addressing the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction; or (c) an offence against the Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011, or a contravention of regulations made under that Act, that is prescribed by regulations made under this Act for the purposes of this paragraph; or (d) the provision of assets (including funds) or financial services, or other dealing with assets, in contravention of a law of the Commonwealth that: (i) implements an international agreement, convention or treaty relating to the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction; and (ii) is prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph; or (e) an offence against a law of a State or Territory that corresponds to an offence referred to in paragraph (a), (b), (c) or (d); or (f) an offence against a law of a foreign country or a part of a paragraph (a), (b), (c), (d) or (e); or (g) an offence against a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory that is prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of business premises and related terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms such as 'reporting entity business premises', 'reporting group', 'representative office', and others. It specifies that business premises include physical or electronic storage areas for business records, and outlines the meaning of terms like 'resident', 'reviewable decision', and 'RSA' as referenced in other acts. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Business premises used wholly or partly for business operations
Business premises used wholly or partly for storage of records

sections.source_quote: reporting entity business premises means: (a) premises, or a part of premises, used wholly or partly for the purposes of the business operations of: (i) a reporting entity; or (ii) an agent of a reporting entity; or (b) premises, or a part of premises, used wholly or partly for the purposes of the storage (whether in electronic form or otherwise) of records relating to the business operations of: (i) a reporting entity; or (ii) an agent of a reporting entity; where the occupier of the premises, or the part of premises, carries on a business of storing records at the premises or the part of premises.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of financial and regulatory terms
sections.summary: This section defines terms such as 'subdivision arrangement', 'subject to a requirement', 'subsidiary', and others. It specifies that a subdivision arrangement involves land division and property management, and outlines the meaning of terms like 'taxation officer' and 'threshold transaction'. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Subdivision arrangement involves land division and property management
Subject to a requirement includes subject to a prohibition

sections.source_quote: subdivision arrangement means: (a) an arrangement: (i) under which the title (whether freehold or leasehold) to a portion of land is subdivided into separate freehold or leasehold titles relating to smaller portions of land within the first-mentioned portion; and (ii) under which property that is common between the owners or occupiers of the smaller portions is owned or managed by a single body corporate (however described); and (iii) that exists under a law, of the State or Territory in which the land is located, relating to "strata titles", "community titles", "unit titles", "cluster titles" or something referred to by another term reflecting the features referred to in subparagraphs (i) and (ii); or (b) an arrangement under which: (i) a body corporate owns an interest (whether freehold or leasehold) in land; and (ii) under the constitution of the body corporate, a holder of shares in the body has, or may be granted, a right to occupy or use some or all of the land, whether the right is by way of a lease or licence or otherwise; or (c) an arrangement in relation to which all of the following subparagraphs apply: (i) under the arrangement, an interest (whether freehold or leasehold) in land is held on trust; (ii) under the terms of the trust, a holder of an interest in the trust has, or may be granted, a right to occupy or use part of the land, whether the right is by way of a lease or licence or otherwise; (iii) the trustee of the trust is a body corporate; (iv) there are at least 2 distinct parts of the land for which subparagraph (ii) is satisfied.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of financial transaction terms
sections.summary: This section defines terms such as 'threshold transaction', 'totalisator agency board', 'transaction', 'transfer', and others. It specifies that a threshold transaction involves physical currency transfers of $10,000 or more, and outlines the meaning of terms like 'taxation officer' and 'virtual asset'. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Threshold transaction involves physical currency transfer of $10,000 or more
Transaction includes non-commercial transactions

sections.source_quote: threshold transaction means: (a) a transaction involving the transfer of physical currency, where the total amount of physical currency transferred is not less than $10,000; or (c) if: (i) the regulations provide that this definition applies to a specified transaction involving money; and (ii) the regulations provide that a specified amount is the transaction threshold for the specified transaction; the specified transaction, where the total amount transferred is not less than the transaction threshold for the transaction; or (ca) if: (i) the regulations provide that this definition applies to a specified transaction involving a virtual asset; and (ii) the regulations provide that a specified amount or value is the transaction threshold for the specified transaction; the specified transaction, where the total amount or value transferred is not less than the transaction threshold for the transaction.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of virtual assets and related terms
sections.summary: This section defines terms such as 'virtual asset', 'virtual asset safekeeping service', 'Virtual Asset Service Provider Register', and others. It specifies that a virtual asset is a digital representation of value that functions as a medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account, or investment, and is not issued by a government body. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Virtual asset is a digital representation of value that functions as a medium of exchange
Virtual asset is a digital representation of value that is not issued by or under the authority of a government body

sections.source_quote: virtual asset has the meaning given by section 5B. virtual asset safekeeping service: (a) means a service in which virtual assets or private keys are controlled or managed for or on behalf of a person (the customer) or another person nominated by the customer under an arrangement between the provider of the service and the customer, or between the provider of the service and another person with whom the customer has an arrangement (whether or not there are also other parties to any such arrangement); but (b) does not include a service of a kind prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of precious metals and stones
sections.summary: This section defines terms such as 'precious metal', 'precious stone', and 'precious product'. It specifies that precious metals include gold, silver, platinum, and others, and precious stones have gem quality and market-recognised beauty, rarity, and value. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Precious metals include gold, silver, platinum, and others
Precious stones have gem quality and market-recognised beauty, rarity, and value

sections.source_quote: Precious metal (1) Each of the following substances is a precious metal: (a) gold; (b) silver; (c) platinum; (d) iridium; (e) osmium; (f) palladium; (g) rhodium; (h) ruthenium; (i) a substance prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules; (j) an alloy substance with at least 2% in weight of any of the substances mentioned in paragraphs (a) to (i).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of designated services

It specifies the types of entities that can provide designated services, including ADIs, banks, credit card issuers, and credit card acquirers. The definitions are primarily for clarity and consistency across the Act.

requirements.text: Designated services include opening accounts, allowing signatories, and conducting transactions

sections.source_quote: Item 1 in the capacity of account provider, opening an account, where the (a) an ADI; or (b) a bank; or (e) a credit card issuer; or (f) a credit card acquirer; or (g) a person specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Loan and finance lease participants in specific financial transactions

It specifies the roles of lenders, assignees, factoring businesses, and lessees under conditions that the transaction occurs in the course of a financial business.

requirements.text: The borrower in a loan transaction where the loan is made in the course of carrying on a loans business
The borrower in a finance lease transaction where the goods are not acquired by a consumer and the supply is in the course of carrying on a finance leasing business
The person in a hire-purchase agreement where the goods are not acquired by a consumer and the supply is in the course of supplying goods

sections.source_quote: 51 Compilation Item 6: making a loan, where the loan is made in the course of carrying on a loans business the borrower

sections.dtia_summary: Identifies key participants in financial transactions that may require data transfer impact assessments for cross-border financial data flows, particularly in account and card services.

requirements.text: The holder of the account with the building society or credit union
The holder of the beneficial interest in the trust
The person for whom a promissory note or letter of credit is issued by an ADI, bank, building society, credit union, or person specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: 53 Compilation Item 14: for an account, providing a chequebook, or a similar facility, that enables the holder of the account to draw a cheque on the account

sections.title: Debit card and stored value card services for specific financial entities

sections.dtia_summary: Defines participants in debit and stored value card transactions that may require data transfer impact assessments for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: The person for whom a debit card is issued by an ADI, bank, or person specified in the AML/CTF Rules
The signatory authorized by the holder of an account to debit an account held by a building society or credit union

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.dtia_summary: Defines participants in stored value card and traveller's cheque transactions that may require data transfer impact assessments for cross-border financial data flows.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Money order and value transfer services

sections.dtia_summary: Identifies participants in money order and value transfer services that may require data transfer impact assessments for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: The person for whom a money order, postal order, or similar order is issued where the face value is not less than $1,000
The payer in the capacity of ordering institution accepting an instruction for the transfer of value
The payee in the capacity of beneficiary institution making the transferred value available

sections.source_quote: 59 Compilation Item 28: in the capacity of issuer of a money order, postal order or similar order, cashing or redeeming a money order, postal order or similar order held by a person, where the face value of the order is not less than: (a) $1,000

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.dtia_summary: Identifies participants in securities and derivative transactions that may require data transfer impact assessments for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: The person for whom a security or derivative is issued where the issue is in the course of carrying on a business of issuing or selling securities or derivatives
The person to whom the proceeds of redemption are paid for a bearer bond
The holder of the policy for life insurance or sinking fund policies

sections.source_quote: 61 Compilation Item 35: issuing or selling a security or derivative to a person, where: (a) the issue or sale is in the course of carrying on a business of issuing or selling securities or derivatives; and (b) in the case of an issue of a security or derivative—the issue does not consist of the issue by a company of either of the following: (i) a security of the company (other than an interest in a managed investment scheme); or (ii) an option to acquire a security of the company (other than an option to acquire an interest in a managed investment scheme)

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in pension/annuity transactions
sections.summary: This section defines specific individuals who may be involved in pension or annuity payments, including the provider of pension/annuity, trustee of superannuation funds, RSA providers, and other entities. It specifies the roles and relationships for different types of transactions such as payments, contributions, and cashing out interests. The section outlines the parties who are considered relevant for regulatory purposes in these financial contexts.

requirements.text: In the capacity of provider of a pension or annuity, making a payment to a person
In the capacity of trustee of a superannuation fund (other than self-managed), accepting a contribution
In the capacity of trustee of a superannuation fund (other than self-managed), cashing out interests
In the capacity of RSA provider, accepting contributions to RSA

sections.source_quote: 63 Compilation Item 41 in the capacity of provider of a pension or annuity, making a payment to a person by way of: (a) a payment of the pension or annuity; or (b) an amount resulting from the commutation, in whole or in part, of the pension or annuity; or (c) the residual capital value of the pension or annuity; where the provider is not a self managed superannuation fund the person

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in currency exchange and virtual asset transactions
sections.summary: This section defines individuals who may be involved in currency exchanges, virtual asset transactions, and other financial services. It specifies the parties responsible for transactions such as exchanging currencies, virtual assets, and providing designated services. The section outlines the roles for different types of financial activities including gambling and virtual asset services.

requirements.text: In the capacity of guarantor of a loan, making a payment to the lender
Exchanging one currency for another in the course of currency exchange business
Exchanging virtual assets for money or money for virtual assets in the course of virtual asset service provider business

sections.source_quote: 65 Compilation Item 49 in the capacity of guarantor of a loan, making a payment to the lender, where the guarantee was given in the course of carrying on a business of guaranteeing loans both: (a) the lender; and (b) the borrower

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in gambling transactions
sections.summary: This section defines individuals who may be involved in gambling transactions, including placing bets, receiving winnings, and operating gaming machines. It specifies the parties responsible for different types of gambling activities such as betting, gaming machines, and prize distribution. The section outlines the roles for transactions that involve games of chance and related services.

requirements.text: Receiving or accepting a bet placed by a person in the course of gambling business
Placing or making a bet on behalf of a person in the course of gambling business

sections.source_quote: 67 Compilation Item 1 in the capacity of controller of an eligible gaming machine venue, allowing a person to play a game on a gaming machine located at the venue, where the service is provided in the course of carrying on a business

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in real estate and financial transactions
sections.summary: This section defines individuals who may be involved in real estate services, financial transactions, and other business activities. It specifies the parties responsible for transactions such as brokering real estate, selling property, and assisting in financial transactions. The section outlines the roles for different types of services including real estate, financial services, and virtual asset transactions.

requirements.text: Brokering the sale, purchase or transfer of real estate on behalf of a buyer, seller, transferee or transferor
Selling or transferring real estate in the course of carrying on a business selling real estate

sections.source_quote: 69 Compilation Item 1 brokering the sale, purchase or transfer of real estate on behalf of a buyer, seller, transferee or transferor both: (a) the seller or transferor; and (b) the buyer or transferee

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in virtual asset and financial services
sections.summary: This section defines individuals who may be involved in virtual asset transactions, financial services, and other business activities. It specifies the parties responsible for transactions such as providing virtual asset services, exchanging currencies, and facilitating financial transactions. The section outlines the roles for different types of services including virtual asset services, real estate, and financial transactions.

requirements.text: Providing a service specified in the regulations
Assisting a person in the planning or execution of a transaction to sell, buy or otherwise transfer real estate

sections.source_quote: 71 Compilation Item 1 providing a service specified in the regulations the person who, under the regulations, is taken to be the person to whom the service is provided

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of persons involved in financial and legal transactions
sections.summary: This section defines individuals who may be involved in financial and legal transactions, including assisting with transactions, managing assets, and facilitating financial services. It specifies the parties responsible for different types of activities such as managing money, securities, and virtual assets. The section outlines the roles for transactions that involve legal arrangements and financial services.

requirements.text: Assisting a person in the planning or execution of a transaction to sell, buy or otherwise transfer real estate
Receiving, holding and controlling money, accounts, securities, virtual assets, or other property as part of assisting a person

sections.source_quote: 73 Compilation Item 3 receiving, holding and controlling (including disbursing) or managing a person's money; accounts; securities and securities accounts; virtual assets; or other property as part of assisting the person in the planning or execution of a transaction

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Designated services for business entities
sections.summary: This section defines specific business activities that constitute designated services for regulatory purposes. It specifies that designated services include roles such as directors, partners, trustees, and equivalent positions in legal arrangements, excluding certain circumstances covered by subsection (5E). The services must be provided in the course of carrying on a business and not fall under specific exemptions outlined in subsection (5C).
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines what constitutes a designated service for regulatory purposes, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving business entities. Designated services are subject to specific reporting requirements under the AML/CTF Rules, affecting how financial data is handled across borders.

requirements.text: A designated service includes a director or secretary of a company
A designated service includes a power of attorney of a body corporate or legal arrangement
A designated service includes a partner in a partnership
A designated service includes a trustee of an express trust
A designated service includes a position in any other legal arrangement functionally equivalent to the above
Designated services must be provided in the course of carrying on a business
Designated services are excluded if the money, accounts, securities, virtual assets or other property are held for payments reasonably incidental to the service
Designated services are excluded if the money, accounts, securities, virtual assets or other property are received or payable under a court order
Designated services are excluded if the service involves receipt or disbursement of payments under subsection (5D)
Designated services are excluded if the service is a designated service specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: 75 Compilation Item service following, on behalf of a person (the nominator), in the course of carrying on a business: (a) a director or secretary of a company; (b) a power of attorney of a body corporate or legal arrangement; (c) a partner in a partnership; (d) a trustee of an express trust; (e) a position in any other legal arrangement that is functionally equivalent to a position mentioned in any of the above paragraphs; other than in a circumstance covered by subsection (5E)

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Geographical scope for designated services
sections.summary: This section specifies the geographical conditions under which a designated service provided by a person to a customer applies to Australia. It requires that the service must be provided at or through a permanent establishment in Australia, or the person must be an Australian resident with a foreign permanent establishment, or a subsidiary of an Australian resident company with a foreign permanent establishment. These conditions determine the applicability of the section to cross-border services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the geographical scope for designated services, which is critical for determining whether cross-border data transfers involving financial services require specific regulatory compliance measures under the AML/CTF framework.

requirements.text: A designated service provided by a person to a customer applies to Australia if the service is provided at or through a permanent establishment in Australia
A designated service provided by a person to a customer applies to Australia if the person is an Australian resident with a foreign permanent establishment
A designated service provided by a person to a customer applies to Australia if the person is a subsidiary of an Australian resident company with a foreign permanent establishment

sections.source_quote: 77 Compilation An item of a table in this section, other than item 32A of table 1, does not apply to the provision by a person of a service to a customer unless: (a) the service is provided at or through a permanent establishment of the person in Australia; or (b) both of the following subparagraphs apply: (i) the person is a resident of Australia; (ii) the service is provided at or through a permanent establishment of the person in a foreign country; or (c) both of the following subparagraphs apply: (i) the person is a subsidiary of a company that is a resident of Australia; (ii) the service is provided at or through a permanent establishment of the person in a foreign country.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Security and derivative definitions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the context of securities and derivatives. It specifies that 'security' has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001, and 'derivative' has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001. The AML/CTF Rules may override these definitions for specific things.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides definitions for securities and derivatives, which are critical for understanding financial data classifications and cross-border transfers involving these assets.

requirements.text: Security has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001
The AML/CTF Rules may provide that a specified thing is a security
Derivative has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2.01
The AML/CTF Rules may provide that a specified thing is not a derivative

sections.source_quote: 79 Compilation 7A Securities and derivatives (1) Security has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001. (2) Despite subsection (1), the AML/CTF Rules may provide: (a) that a specified thing is a security; or (b) that a specified thing is not a security. (3) Derivative has the same meaning as in Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act 2001. (4) Despite subsection (3), the AML/CTF Rules may provide: (a) that a specified thing is a derivative; or (b) that a specified thing is not a derivative.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting groups and business groups
sections.summary: This section defines reporting groups and business groups for regulatory purposes. A reporting group consists of a business group meeting specific conditions or a group of persons who have elected to be members. Business groups are defined as groups where one person controls others or meets AML/CTF Rules requirements. These definitions impact how cross-border financial services are regulated.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines reporting groups and business groups, which are critical for determining the scope of cross-border data transfers involving financial services and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: A reporting group is a business group where at least one person provides a designated service
A reporting group is a business group where each member satisfies conditions specified in the AML/CTF Rules
A reporting group is a business group where the group is not ineligible under the AML/CTF Rules
A reporting group is a group of 2 or more persons where each member has elected to be a member
A business group is a group of 2 or more persons where one person controls others
A business group is a group of 2 or more persons meeting AML/CTF Rules requirements

sections.source_quote: 81 Compilation Members of reporting groups and business groups (4) Each person in a reporting group or a business group is a member of that group. Lead entity of a reporting group (5) The lead entity of a reporting group means the person in the group that is specified in the AML/CTF Rules as the lead entity for the reporting group. 11 Meaning of control (1) Control, of a body corporate, is: (a) having the capacity to cast, or control the casting of, more than one half of the maximum number of votes that might be cast at a general meeting of the body corporate; or (b) directly or indirectly holding more than one half of the issued share capital of the body corporate (not including any part of the issued share capital that carries no right to participate beyond a specified amount in a distribution of either profits or capital, and not including mutual capital instruments within the meaning of section 167AD of the Corporations Act 2001); or (c) having the capacity to control the composition of the body corporate's board or governing body; or (d) having the capacity to determine the outcome of decisions about the body corporate's financial and operating policies, taking into account: (i) the practical influence that can be exerted (rather than the rights that can be enforced); and (ii) any practice or pattern of behaviour affecting the body corporate's financial or operating policies (whether or not it involves a breach of an agreement or a breach of trust). (2) Control, of a person other than a body corporate, is: (a) having the capacity to control the composition of the person's board or governing body (if any); or (b) having the capacity to determine the outcome of decisions about the person's financial and operating policies, taking into account: (i) the practical influence that can be exerted (rather than the rights that can be enforced); and (ii) any practice or pattern of behaviour affecting the person's financial or operating policies (whether or not it involves a breach of an agreement or a breach of trust).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Residency definitions for entities
sections.summary: This section defines residency for individuals, companies, trusts, partnerships, and corporations sole. Residency is determined based on ordinary residence for individuals, incorporation for companies, trustee residency for trusts, and partner residency for partnerships. These definitions are critical for determining the geographical scope of regulatory compliance for cross-border financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines residency for various entities, which is essential for determining the geographical scope of cross-border data transfers and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: An individual is a resident of a country if they are ordinarily resident in that country
A company is a resident of a country if it is incorporated in that country
A company is a resident of a country if an individual controls it and that individual is a resident of the country
A trust is a resident of a country if the trustee is a resident of that country
A trust is a resident of a country if an individual controls it and that individual is a resident of the country
A trust is a resident of a country if a person benefits from it and that person is a resident of the country
A partnership is a resident of a country if a partner is a resident of that country
A corporation sole is a resident of a country if it was established in that country

sections.source_quote: 83 Compilation 14 Residency Individual (1) For the purposes of this Act, an individual (including an individual in the capacity of trustee) is a resident of a particular country if, and only if, the individual is ordinarily resident in that country. Note: See also subsections (7), (8) and (9). Company (2) For the purposes of this Act, a company (including a company in the capacity of trustee) is a resident of a particular country if, and only if: (a) the company is incorporated in that country; or (b) both: (i) an individual controls the company; and (ii) the individual is a resident of that country. Trust (3) For the purposes of this Act, a trust is a resident of a particular country if, and only if: (a) the trustee, or any of the trustees, is a resident of that country; or (b) both: (i) an individual controls the trust; and (ii) the individual is a resident of that country; or (c) both: (i) a person benefits or is capable (whether by the exercise of a power of appointment or otherwise) of benefiting under the trust, either directly or through any interposed companies, partnerships or trusts; and (ii) the person is a resident of that country. Partnership (4) For the purposes of this Act, a partnership is a resident of a particular country if, and only if, a partner is a resident of that country. Corporation sole (5) For the purposes of this Act, a corporation sole is a resident of a particular country if, and only if, the corporation sole was established in that country. Body politic (6) For the purposes of this Act, a body politic of, or of a part of, a particular country is a resident of that country.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Electronic communications and negotiable instruments
sections.summary: This section defines electronic communications and negotiable instruments for regulatory purposes. It states that electronic communications do not affect the application of the Act, and specifies that bearer negotiable instruments include cheques, promissory notes, bearer bonds, traveller's cheques, money orders, and similar orders. These definitions are critical for understanding how financial transactions are classified and regulated.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides definitions for electronic communications and negotiable instruments, which are essential for classifying financial data and determining regulatory compliance for cross-border transactions.

requirements.text: Electronic communications do not affect the application of the Act
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a cheque
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a promissory note
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a bearer bond
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a traveller's cheque
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a money order, postal order or similar order
A bearer negotiable instrument includes a negotiable instrument not covered by the above

sections.source_quote: 85 Compilation 16 Electronic communications (1) Unless the contrary intention appears, in determining the application of a provision of this Act, it is immaterial whether any act or thing is or was done wholly or partly by means of one or more electronic communications. (2) Subsection (1) is enacted for the avoidance of doubt.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of officials in Commonwealth, State or Territory agencies
sections.summary: This section defines who constitutes an official of a Commonwealth, State, or Territory agency for the purposes of the Act. It includes heads, members, staff, officers, employees, and individuals under the head's direction, as well as legal practitioners and authorized persons in royal commissions. The definition also specifies that royal commissions include legal practitioners appointed to assist the Commission and persons authorized under subsection (3).

requirements.text: The head (however described) of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency
A member or acting member of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency
A member of the staff of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency
An officer or employee of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency
An individual under the head (however described) of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency
An individual taken to be an official under the AML/CTF Rules
A legal practitioner (however described) appointed to assist the Commission
A person authorized under subsection (3)

sections.source_quote: 87 (1) For the purposes of this Act, an official of a Commonwealth, State or Territory agency covered by paragraph (a), (b), (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (h), (i) or (j) of the definition of Commonwealth, State or Territory agency in section 5 is: (a) the head (however described) of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency; or (b) a member or acting member of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency; or (c) a member of the staff of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency; or (d) an officer or employee of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency; or (e) an officer, employee or other individual under the direction of the head (however described) of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency; or (f) an individual who, under the AML/CTF Rules, is taken to be an official of the Commonwealth, State or Territory agency for the purposes of this Act; and, in the case of a Commonwealth Royal Commission or a State/Territory Royal Commission, includes the following: (g) a legal practitioner (however described) appointed to assist the Commission; (h) a person authorised under subsection (3).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Extension of the Act to external territories
sections.summary: This section states that the Act extends to every external territory of Australia. It does not specify any conditions or exceptions for this extension, making it a straightforward territorial application provision.

requirements.text: This Act extends to every external Territory

sections.source_quote: 89 This Act extends to every external Territory.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Simplified outline of AML/CTF programs
sections.summary: This section provides a simplified outline of what constitutes an AML/CTF program for reporting entities. It requires that an AML/CTF program includes a ML/TF risk assessment and AML/CTF policies that are appropriate to the entity's business size, complexity, and nature. The program must be managed by the governing body and include a compliance officer with oversight responsibilities.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must have and comply with an AML/CTF program
An AML/CTF program comprises the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment and AML/CTF policies
The ML/TF risk assessment is an assessment of the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing
The AML/CTF policies must appropriately manage and mitigate those risks
The AML/CTF program must be appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the reporting entity's business
For a lead entity of a reporting group, the program must be appropriate to the business of each reporting entity in the group
The governing body has responsibilities relating to the AML/CTF program
The reporting entity must have an AML/CTF compliance officer

sections.source_quote: 26A A reporting entity must have and comply with an AML/CTF program. An AML/CTF program comprises the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment and AML/CTF policies.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of AML/CTF program
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes an AML/CTF program for a reporting entity. It specifies that an AML/CTF program consists of the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment and AML/CTF policies. This definition is critical for understanding the components required for compliance with the Act.

requirements.text: An AML/CTF program of a reporting entity comprises: (a) the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment; and (b) the reporting entity's AML/CTF policies

sections.source_quote: 91 An AML/CTF program of a reporting entity comprises: (a) the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment; and (b) the reporting entity's AML/CTF policies.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting entities must undertake an ML/TF risk assessment
sections.summary: This section mandates that reporting entities must conduct an ML/TF risk assessment to identify and assess the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism, and proliferation financing they may face in providing designated services. The assessment must be appropriate to the entity's business size, complexity, and nature, and must consider factors such as the types of services, customers, delivery channels, countries involved, and AUSTRAC communications.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must undertake an assessment (an ML/TF risk assessment) that identifies and assesses the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing
The steps taken by a reporting entity in relation to undertaking the ML/TF risk assessment must be appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the reporting entity's business

sections.source_quote: 26C A reporting entity must undertake an assessment (an ML/TF risk assessment) that identifies and assesses the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that the reporting entity may reasonably face in providing its designated services.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting entities must review and update ML/TF risk assessment
sections.summary: This section requires reporting entities to review and update their ML/TF risk assessments periodically to identify and assess new or changed risks. Reviews must occur at least once every three years, or when significant changes occur in the entity's business, AUSTRAC communications, or circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must review its ML/TF risk assessment for the purpose of identifying and assessing any new or changed risks of providing its designated services
The review must be undertaken at least once every 3 years

sections.source_quote: 26D A reporting entity must review its ML/TF risk assessment for the purpose of identifying and assessing any new or changed risks of providing its designated services: (a) if any of the following occur: (i) there is a significant change to any of the matters mentioned in subsection 26C(3); (ii) AUSTRAC communicates to the reporting entity information that identifies or assesses risks associated with the reporting entity's provision of its designated services; (iii) circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules; and (b) in any event—at least once every 3 years.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ML/TF risk assessment updates after reviews
sections.summary: Reporting entities must update their money laundering and terrorist financing risk assessments within specific timeframes following reviews. For significant changes within their control, updates must occur before the change happens. For other issues, updates must be completed as soon as practicable after the review. Non-compliance with these update requirements constitutes a contravention of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely updates to risk assessments after internal reviews to ensure ongoing compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations during data transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must update ML/TF risk assessment before significant change occurs
Reporting entity must update ML/TF risk assessment as soon as practicable after review completion

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must update its ML/TF risk assessment to address any issues identified by a review: (a) for a significant change that is within the control of the reporting entity—before the significant change occurs; or (b) in any other case—as soon as practicable after the review is completed.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF policy development and maintenance
sections.summary: Reporting entities must develop and maintain comprehensive AML/CTF policies that address money laundering and terrorist financing risks, ensure compliance with the Act and regulations, and are appropriate to their business size and complexity. These policies must include customer due diligence, risk assessment updates, and regular reviews as specified in the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific AML/CTF policies that must be maintained to ensure compliance with international financial regulations during cross-border data transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must develop and maintain AML/CTF policies that manage money laundering and terrorist financing risks
Reporting entity must ensure compliance with obligations imposed by the Act, regulations and AML/CTF Rules
AML/CTF policies must be appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the reporting entity's business
AML/CTF policies must comply with any requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must develop and maintain policies, procedures, systems and controls (AML/CTF policies) that: (a) appropriately manage and mitigate the risks of money that the reporting entity may reasonably face in providing its designated services; and (b) ensure the reporting entity complies with the obligations imposed by this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules on the reporting entity; and (c) are appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the reporting entity's business; and (d) comply with any requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF policy content requirements
sections.summary: AML/CTF policies must include specific elements such as identifying significant changes, conducting customer due diligence, reviewing policies in response to risk assessments, and ensuring regular reviews at least once every three years. These policies must also address the appointment of compliance officers, senior managers responsible for approvals, employee due diligence, and training requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies detailed content requirements for AML/CTF policies that must be maintained to ensure robust compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations during international data transfers.

requirements.text: AML/CTF policies must deal with identifying significant changes to matters mentioned in subsection 26C(3)
AML/CTF policies must carry out customer due diligence in accordance with Part 2
AML/CTF policies must review and update in response to ML/TF risk assessment reviews
AML/CTF policies must review at least once every 3 years
AML/CTF policies must designate an AML/CTF compliance officer
AML/CTF policies must designate senior managers responsible for approving AML/CTF policies and ML/TF risk assessments
AML/CTF policies must undertake due diligence for employees performing relevant functions
AML/CTF policies must provide training on money laundering risks and obligations

sections.source_quote: Without limiting paragraph (1)(a), the AML/CTF policies of a reporting entity must deal with the following: (a) identifying significant changes to any of the matters mentioned in subsection 26C(3); (b) carrying out customer due diligence in accordance with Part 2; (c) reviewing and updating the AML/CTF policies in the following circumstances: (i) in response to a review of the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment under section 26D; (ii) circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules; (d) reviewing the AML/CTF policies of the reporting entity at the intervals or with the frequency specified in the AML/CTF Rules (and in any event at least once every 3 years); (e) any other matters specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting group information sharing requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities that lead reporting groups must ensure appropriate information sharing between group members for customer due diligence, risk assessment, and compliance purposes. This includes identifying, assessing, and mitigating money laundering and terrorist financing risks across the group, as well as ensuring that obligations are discharged by members without duplication of efforts.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting entities to share information within reporting groups to ensure consistent compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations across international data transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Lead entity must ensure appropriate sharing of information between reporting group members for customer due diligence
Lead entity must ensure appropriate sharing of information between reporting group members for risk assessment and mitigation

sections.source_quote: Without limiting paragraph (1)(a), if a reporting entity is the lead entity of a reporting group, the AML/CTF policies of the reporting entity must deal with the following: (a) ensuring the appropriate sharing of information between members of the reporting group for the following purposes: (i) carrying out customer due diligence under Part 2; (ii) appropriately identifying, assessing, managing and mitigating the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that each reporting entity that is a member of the reporting group may reasonably face in providing its designated services;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting group information sharing implementation
sections.summary: Reporting entities that lead reporting groups must ensure information sharing between group members for compliance purposes, including specifying which obligations members can discharge, ensuring access to records demonstrating obligation discharge, and maintaining confidentiality of shared information to prevent contraventions of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting entities to implement specific information sharing mechanisms within reporting groups to ensure consistent compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations during international data transfers.

requirements.text: Lead entity must ensure sharing of information between reporting group members for compliance purposes
Lead entity must specify which members discharge which obligations
Lead entity must ensure each member has access to records demonstrating obligation discharge
Lead entity must ensure confidentiality and appropriate use of shared information

sections.source_quote: Without limiting paragraph (1)(b), if a reporting entity is the lead entity of a reporting group, the AML/CTF policies of the reporting entity must deal with the following: (a) ensuring the sharing of information between members of the reporting group that is necessary for the members of the reporting group who are reporting entities to comply with: (i) their obligations imposed by this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules; and (ii) the AML/CTF policies of the lead entity;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting group compliance with AML/CTF policies
sections.summary: Reporting entities must comply with the AML/CTF policies of the lead entity in reporting groups. This includes ensuring that all members follow the lead entity's policies for compliance with the Act, regulations, and AML/CTF rules, as well as maintaining appropriate documentation for their obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates that reporting entities within groups must comply with the lead entity's AML/CTF policies to ensure consistent application of anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing obligations across international data transfers.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must comply with AML/CTF policies of the lead entity in reporting groups

sections.source_quote: If a reporting entity is a member of a reporting group and is not the lead entity of the reporting group, the reporting entity must also comply with the AML/CTF policies of the lead entity of the reporting group that apply to the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF policy compliance for reporting entities
sections.summary: The lead entity of a reporting group must comply with its own AML/CTF policies. Subsections (1) and (2) are civil penalty provisions that apply to contraventions of this section.

requirements.text: The lead entity of the reporting group must comply with its own AML/CTF policies

sections.source_quote: The lead entity of the reporting group must comply with its own AML/CTF policies under subsection (1).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Governing bodies' oversight of AML/CTF compliance
sections.summary: Governing bodies must exercise ongoing oversight of a reporting entity's risk assessment and compliance with AML/CTF policies. They must take reasonable steps to ensure the entity identifies, assesses, manages and mitigates money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks, and complies with relevant laws and policies.

requirements.text: Exercise appropriate ongoing oversight of the reporting entity's identification and assessment of risk for ML/TF risk assessment
Take reasonable steps to ensure the reporting entity is appropriately identifying, assessing, managing and mitigating money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks
Take reasonable steps to ensure the reporting entity complies with AML/CTF policies, the Act, regulations and AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: The governing body of a reporting entity must: (a) exercise appropriate ongoing oversight of: (i) the reporting entity's identification and assessment of risk for the purposes of its ML/TF risk assessment; and (ii) the reporting entity's compliance with its AML/CTF policies, the Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules; and (b) take reasonable steps to ensure that the reporting entity: (i) is appropriately identifying, assessing, managing and mitigating the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that the reporting entity may reasonably face in providing its designated services; and (ii) is otherwise complying with its AML/CTF policies, the Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Designation of AML/CTF compliance officers
sections.summary: Reporting entities must designate an individual as their AML/CTF compliance officer who is employed at management level, has sufficient authority and access to resources, and is a fit and proper person resident in Australia.

requirements.text: Designate an individual as the AML/CTF compliance officer for the reporting entity
Ensure the designated individual is employed or otherwise engaged by the reporting entity at management level
Ensure the designated individual has sufficient authority, independence and access to resources and information
Ensure the designated individual is a resident of Australia
Ensure the designated individual is a fit and proper person

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must designate an individual as the compliance officer (the AML/CTF compliance officer) for the AML/CTF compliance officers must have sufficient authority etc.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalties for AML/CTF compliance officer designation
sections.summary: Failure to designate an eligible AML/CTF compliance officer within the required timeframe constitutes a contravention subject to civil penalties. The obligation continues until a compliant officer is designated or the entity ceases to be a reporting entity.

requirements.text: A reporting entity contravenes this subsection if the reporting entity designates an individual as its AML/CTF compliance officer and the individual is not eligible under subsection (3) to be designated

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity contravenes this subsection if: (a) the reporting entity designates an individual as its AML/CTF compliance officer; and (b) the individual is not eligible under subsection (3) to be designated as the AML/CTF compliance officer for the reporting entity

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF compliance officer designation timelines
sections.summary: Reporting entities must designate an AML/CTF compliance officer within 28 days of commencing a designated service or when an existing officer becomes ineligible. Failure to comply results in ongoing obligations until a compliant officer is designated or the entity ceases to be a reporting entity.

requirements.text: Designate an individual as the AML/CTF compliance officer within 28 days after commencing a designated service
Designate another individual as the AML/CTF compliance officer within 28 days after an existing officer ceases to be eligible

sections.source_quote: If: (a) a reporting entity commences to provide a designated service; and (b) an individual is not designated as the AML/CTF compliance officer for the reporting entity; the reporting entity must, no later than 28 days after the day on which the reporting entity commences to provide the designated service, designate an individual as the AML/CTF compliance officer for the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF compliance officer notification to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: Reporting entities must notify AUSTRAC of their designated AML/CTF compliance officer within 14 days of designation. The notice must be in the approved form and include required information and supporting documents.

requirements.text: Notify AUSTRAC of the individual designated as the reporting entity's AML/CTF compliance officer within 14 days after designation
Ensure the notice is in the approved form
Include required information and supporting documents with the notice

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must notify AUSTRAC of the individual who is designated as the reporting entity's AML/CTF compliance officer within 14 days after the individual is designated as the AML/CTF compliance officer for the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF program documentation and approvals requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must document their AML/CTF programs and related matters within specified periods. The documentation must include the AML/CTF program itself and any other matters specified in the AML/CTF Rules. Reporting entities must comply with these documentation requirements. The program may be documented together with a registered scheme's compliance plan if the entity is a responsible entity of a registered scheme.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires documentation of AML/CTF programs for cross-border financial entities, impacting data transfer assessments for financial institutions handling money laundering risks.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must document its AML/CTF program within the period specified in the AML/CTF Rules
A reporting entity must document any other matter relating to the AML/CTF program specified in the AML/CTF Rules
A reporting entity must comply with the documentation requirements
A reporting entity's AML/CTF program may be documented in the same document as the registered scheme's compliance plan

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must document the following, within the period (if any) specified in the AML/CTF Rules: (a) its AML/CTF program; (b) any other matter relating to the AML/CTF program of the reporting entity specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF documentation requests by AUSTRAC
sections.summary: AUSTRAC CEO can request reporting entities to produce AML/CTF documentation within specified periods. Reporting entities must comply with such written notices. This section establishes the authority and procedure for AUSTRAC to request documentation from reporting entities.
sections.dtia_summary: AUSTRAC's ability to request documentation affects data transfer assessments for financial entities, requiring compliance with documentation standards for cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC CEO may request a reporting entity to produce one or more documents required by subsection 26N(1) within the period specified in the notice
A reporting entity must comply with a written notice from AUSTRAC CEO

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO may, by written notice, request a reporting entity to produce one or more of the documents required by subsection 26N(1) within the period specified in the notice.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC's requirement for AML/CTF program updates
