sections.summary: If AUSTRAC CEO determines a reporting entity lacks an AML/CTF program, has outdated programs, or fails to properly manage risks, the entity must undertake risk assessments, update policies, and document these changes within a specified timeframe. This section mandates corrective actions for entities not meeting AML/CTF standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting entities to update AML/CTF programs when risks are identified, impacting data transfer assessments for financial institutions handling cross-border transactions.

requirements.text: If a reporting entity lacks an AML/CTF program, it must undertake and document an ML/TF risk assessment
If a reporting entity has outdated AML/CTF programs, it must review and update the ML/TF risk assessment
If a reporting entity's AML/CTF program does not appropriately identify, assess, manage or mitigate risks, it must develop and document AML/CTF policies
If a reporting entity's AML/CTF program does not appropriately identify, assess, manage or mitigate risks, it must review and update AML/CTF policies
A reporting entity must provide documentation within a specified timeframe

sections.source_quote: This section applies if the AUSTRAC CEO is satisfied that: (a) a reporting entity does not have an AML/CTF program; or (b) the AML/CTF program of a reporting entity is not up to date; or (c) the AML/CTF program of a reporting entity does not appropriately identify, assess, manage or mitigate the risk of providing its designated services.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF program for remittance affiliates
sections.summary: Registered remittance network providers must make their AML/CTF programs available to registered remittance affiliates. Remittance affiliates can undertake their own risk assessments and develop policies without violating this requirement, provided senior managers approve the network provider's risk assessments and policies.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires remittance network providers to share AML/CTF programs with affiliates, impacting data transfer assessments for cross-border remittance services.

requirements.text: A registered remittance network provider must make available an AML/CTF program to its registered remittance affiliates
Remittance affiliates may undertake risk assessments for themselves
Remittance affiliates may develop AML/CTF policies for themselves
Senior managers of remittance affiliates must approve the network provider's ML/TF risk assessment and AML/CTF policies

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity that is a registered remittance network provider must make available an AML/CTF program to its registered remittance affiliates.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Application of AML/CTF rules to financial services licensees
sections.summary: This section applies to financial services licensees providing designated services covered by item 54 of table 1 in section 6. It modifies the application of AML/CTF policies to focus on customer due diligence and risk assessment for financial services. Certain provisions do not apply to these entities, including specific policy requirements and risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines how AML/CTF rules apply to financial services licensees, impacting data transfer assessments for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: This section applies if all designated services provided by a reporting entity are covered by item 54 of table 1 in section 6
Paragraph 26F(1)(a) applies as if it required policies, procedures, systems and controls for initial customer due diligence
Paragraph 26F(1)(b) applies as if it required policies, procedures, systems and controls appropriate to money laundering risks
This Part does not apply to the reporting entity for paragraphs 26F(1)(b) and (3)(a) to (d) and subsection 26F(4)
This Part does not apply to the reporting entity for section 26H
This Part does not apply to the reporting entity for subsection 26P(2)
This Part does not apply to the reporting entity for Division 5

sections.source_quote: This section applies if all of the designated services provided by a reporting entity are covered by item 54 of table 1 in section 6.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: General exemptions for AML/CTF programs
sections.summary: This Part does not apply to certain designated services, including those of a kind that are exempt from the AML/CTF program requirements. The exemptions cover specific types of services and activities that do not require the full application of the AML/CTF program.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides exemptions for certain designated services from AML/CTF program requirements, which may impact data transfer assessments for specific financial services.

requirements.text: This Part does not apply to a designated service of a kind that is exempt

sections.source_quote: This Part does not apply to a designated service that is of a kind

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Customer due diligence requirements for financial services
sections.summary: Reporting entities must conduct initial and ongoing customer due diligence before and during providing designated services. Initial due diligence may be delayed in special cases, and simplified or enhanced due diligence applies based on risk levels. Pre-commencement customers require modified due diligence, and exemptions exist for specific circumstances.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires establishing customer identity, risk assessment, and KYC information before service provision, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial services.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must undertake initial customer due diligence before providing a designated service to a customer
A reporting entity must undertake ongoing customer due diligence in relation to the provision by the reporting entity of designated services
Simplified customer due diligence may be undertaken in certain low risk circumstances as part of initial and ongoing customer due diligence
Enhanced customer due diligence must be undertaken in certain circumstances as part of initial and ongoing customer due diligence
Certain pre-commencement customers are subject to modified customer due diligence
Exemptions from initial customer due diligence, and ongoing customer due diligence, apply in certain circumstances

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must undertake initial customer due diligence before providing a designated service to a customer. However, in special cases, initial customer due diligence may be carried out after the provision of the designated service.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Initial customer identity verification requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must establish customer identity and related information before commencing services. This includes verifying identity, beneficial ownership, political exposure status, and business purpose. The entity must assess money laundering and terrorism financing risks based on available KYC information and verify information using reliable data sources.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed customer identity verification before data transfer initiation, impacting cross-border financial data flows with specific identity and risk assessment requirements.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must not commence to provide a designated service to a customer if the reporting entity has not established on reasonable grounds each of the matters in subsection (2) in relation to the customer

The matters are: identity of customer, identity of person on behalf of customer, identity of person acting on behalf of customer with authority, beneficial owners of customer (if not individual), political exposure status, targeted sanctions status, business purpose, and other specified matters
A reporting entity must take reasonable steps to establish that the customer is the person the customer claims to be
A reporting entity must identify the ML/TF risk of the customer based on KYC information reasonably available
A reporting entity must collect KYC information appropriate to the ML/TF risk of the customer
A reporting entity must verify KYC information using reliable and independent data

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must not commence to provide a designated service to a customer if the reporting entity has not established on reasonable grounds each of the matters in subsection (2) in relation to the customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Customer risk assessment and verification procedures
sections.summary: Reporting entities must assess and verify customer risk levels for money laundering and terrorism financing. This includes identifying ML/TF risk based on available information, collecting relevant KYC data, and verifying information through reliable sources. Additional factors include the reporting entity's risk assessment, customer type, service types, delivery channels, and countries involved.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive risk assessment and verification of customer data before transfer, impacting cross-border financial data flows with specific risk evaluation and validation requirements.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must take reasonable steps to establish that the customer is the person the customer claims to be
A reporting entity must identify the ML/TF risk of the customer based on KYC information reasonably available
A reporting entity must collect KYC information appropriate to the ML/TF risk of the customer
A reporting entity must verify KYC information using reliable and independent data
A reporting entity must take into account the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment when identifying customer ML/TF risk
A reporting entity must consider the kind of customer, designated services, delivery channels, and countries involved when assessing customer ML/TF risk
A reporting entity may take into account matters specified in the AML/CTF Rules when identifying customer ML/TF risk

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must take into account the following matters when identifying the ML/TF risk of the customer for the purposes of paragraph (3)(b): the reporting entity's ML/TF risk assessment, the kind of customer, the kinds of designated services, delivery channels, countries with which the reporting entity deals, and matters specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for initial customer due diligence
sections.summary: Reporting entities may commence services before completing initial customer due diligence under specific conditions. These include circumstances specified in AML/CTF Rules, determining that service commencement is essential to avoid business disruption, having AML/CTF policies to comply with due diligence, assessing low risk of additional ML/TF issues, implementing risk mitigation policies, and complying with specified timeframes.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows temporary exemptions from initial due diligence for critical business continuity, but requires specific risk assessments and mitigation measures for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: A reporting entity may commence to provide a designated service to a customer before the reporting entity complies with subsection 28(1) if circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules apply
The reporting entity determines on reasonable grounds that commencing to provide the designated service is essential to avoid interrupting the ordinary course of business
The reporting entity has AML/CTF policies to comply with subsection 28(1) as soon as reasonably practicable after commencing service
The reporting entity has AML/CTF policies to comply with subsection 28(1) within the period specified in the AML/CTF Rules
The reporting entity determines on reasonable grounds that additional risk of ML/TF is low after commencing service
The reporting entity implements AML/CTF policies to mitigate and manage associated risks

sections.source_quote: Despite subsection 28(1), a reporting entity may commence to provide a designated service to a customer before the reporting entity complies with that subsection if: circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules apply; the reporting entity determines on reasonable grounds that commencing to provide the designated service is essential to avoid interrupting the ordinary course of business; and the reporting entity has AML/CTF policies to comply with subsection 28(1) in relation to the customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ongoing customer monitoring requirements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must monitor customers to identify, assess, manage and mitigate money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks. This includes monitoring for unusual transactions, reviewing ML/TF risk assessments when significant changes occur, and periodically verifying KYC information based on risk levels.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires continuous monitoring of customer activity for suspicious transactions, impacting cross-border data transfers with ongoing risk assessment and verification obligations.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must monitor its customers to identify, assess, manage and mitigate ML/TF risks
A reporting entity must monitor for unusual transactions and behaviors that may give rise to suspicious matter reporting obligations
A reporting entity must review and update ML/TF risk assessments when significant changes occur
A reporting entity must review and update KYC information at a frequency appropriate to the ML/TF risk of the customer

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must monitor its customers in relation to the provision of its designated services to appropriately identify, assess, manage and mitigate the risks of money laundering, financing of terrorism and proliferation financing that the reporting entity may reasonably face in providing designated services.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ongoing customer due diligence triggers for high-risk customers
sections.summary: Reporting entities must monitor for significant changes in business relationships that could elevate a customer's money laundering or terrorist financing risk to medium or high levels. They must also verify KYC information when doubts arise about its adequacy or veracity. Additionally, reporting entities must comply with any other requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules. These obligations apply to reporting entities engaged in designated services with customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ongoing monitoring of business relationships to detect risk escalation, triggering additional due diligence for high-risk customers during cross-border transactions.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: Monitor for significant changes in the nature and purpose of the business relationship that may result in the ML/TF risk of the customer being medium or high
Verify KYC information when doubts exist about its adequacy or veracity
Comply with any other requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: 118 (i) the reporting entity has doubts about the adequacy or veracity of the KYC information relating to the customer; (ii) circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules; and (d) if the reporting entity has a business relationship with a customer that is a pre-commencement customer—monitor for significant changes in the nature and purpose of the business relationship that may result in the ML/TF risk of the customer being medium or high; and (e) comply with any other requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of unusual transactions for customer due diligence
sections.summary: Unusual transactions include unusually large or complex transactions, transactions that form part of an unusual pattern, transactions with no apparent economic purpose, and transactions inconsistent with the reporting entity's knowledge of the customer's background, business purpose, risk level, or source of funds. These definitions guide when enhanced due diligence is required for high-risk customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines what constitutes unusual transactions to trigger enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers during cross-border data transfers.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: Unusually large or complex transactions relating to the customer
Transactions and behaviours that are part of an unusual pattern of transactions and behaviours relating to the customer
Transactions and behaviours that have no apparent economic or lawful purpose
Transactions and behaviours inconsistent with what the reporting entity reasonably knows about the customer, business relationship, ML/TF risk, or source of funds

sections.source_quote: 119 (b) transactions and behaviours that are part of an unusual pattern of transactions and behaviours relating to the customer; (c) transactions and behaviours that have no apparent economic or lawful purpose; (d) transactions and behaviours that are inconsistent with what the reporting entity reasonably knows about any of the following: (i) the customer; (ii) the nature and purpose of the business relationship; (iii) the ML/TF risk of the customer; (iv) where relevant, the customer's source of funds or source of wealth.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Simplified and enhanced customer due diligence measures
sections.summary: Reporting entities may apply simplified customer due diligence measures if the customer's ML/TF risk is low and enhanced due diligence is not required. This section outlines the conditions under which simplified measures can be used, ensuring appropriate risk-based approaches for low-risk customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows simplified due diligence for low-risk customers, reducing compliance burden while maintaining regulatory oversight.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: The ML/TF risk of the customer is low
Section 32 does not apply to the customer
The reporting entity complies with the requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: 120 In complying with the obligation imposed on a reporting entity under subsection 28(1) or 30(1) in relation to a customer, the reporting entity may apply simplified customer due diligence measures if: (a) the ML/TF risk of the customer is low; and (b) section 32 does not apply to the customer; and (c) the reporting entity complies with the requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enhanced customer due diligence triggers for high-risk customers
sections.summary: Reporting entities must apply enhanced customer due diligence measures for customers with high ML/TF risk, suspicious matter reporting obligations, foreign politically exposed persons, individuals in high-risk jurisdictions, or nested services relationships. These triggers ensure robust risk management for high-risk customers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers to mitigate cross-border money laundering and terrorist financing risks.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: The ML/TF risk of the customer is high
A suspicious matter reporting obligation arises for the reporting entity in relation to the customer
The reporting entity proposes to continue to provide a designated service or designated services to the customer
The customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is a foreign politically exposed person
The customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is an individual physically present in a high-risk jurisdiction
The customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is a body corporate or legal arrangement formed in a high-risk jurisdiction
The designated service provided or proposed to be provided to the customer is part of a nested services relationship
The customer is of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: 121 In complying with the obligation imposed on a reporting entity under subsection 28(1) or 30(1) in relation to a customer, the reporting entity must apply enhanced customer due diligence measures appropriate to the ML/TF risk of the customer if one or more of the following apply to the customer: (a) the ML/TF risk of the customer is high; (b) if: (i) a suspicious matter reporting obligation arises for the reporting entity in relation to the customer; and (ii) the reporting entity proposes to continue to provide a designated service or designated services to the customer; (c) the customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is a foreign politically exposed person; (d) the customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is: (i) an individual who is physically present in a high risk jurisdiction for which the international body known as the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence to be applied; or (ii) a body corporate or legal arrangement that was formed in a high risk jurisdiction for which the international body known as the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence to be applied; (e) the designated service provided or proposed to be provided to the customer is provided or proposed to be provided as part of a nested services relationship; (f) the customer is of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Customer due diligence for high-risk jurisdictions
sections.summary: Reporting entities must apply enhanced due diligence for customers in high-risk jurisdictions as defined by the Financial Action Task Force. This includes individuals and entities formed in jurisdictions requiring enhanced due diligence under international standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires enhanced due diligence for customers in high-risk jurisdictions to address cross-border money laundering and terrorist financing risks.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: The customer is an individual physically present in a high-risk jurisdiction for which the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence
The customer is a body corporate or legal arrangement formed in a high-risk jurisdiction for which the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence

sections.source_quote: 122 The customer, any beneficial owner of the customer, any person on whose behalf the customer is receiving the customer, is: (i) an individual who is physically present in a high risk jurisdiction for which the international body known as the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence to be applied; or (ii) a body corporate or legal arrangement that was formed in a high risk jurisdiction for which the international body known as the Financial Action Task Force has called for enhanced due diligence to be applied;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Identity verification through credit reporting bodies
sections.summary: Reporting entities may disclose customer personal information to credit reporting bodies for identity verification purposes, provided they give the customer information about the request, the personal information to be disclosed, and the assessment process. This facilitates identity verification while maintaining privacy protections.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows sharing of customer identity information with credit reporting bodies for verification, requiring transparency and consent.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)

requirements.text: Disclose the individual's name, residential address, and date of birth to a credit reporting body for identity verification
Request the credit reporting body to assess whether the disclosed personal information matches information held by the credit reporting body
Provide the individual with information about the reasons for the request, personal information to be disclosed, and the assessment process

sections.source_quote: 123 (1) A reporting entity may, to assist in verifying the identity of an individual for the purposes of this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules: (a) disclose any or all of the following personal information to a credit reporting body for the purposes of making a request referred to in paragraph (b): (i) the individual's name; (ii) the individual's residential address; (iii) the individual's date of birth; and (b) request the credit reporting body to provide an assessment of whether the personal information so disclosed matches (in whole or part) personal information held by the credit reporting body.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Credit reporting bodies' use of personal information for identity verification assessments
sections.summary: A credit reporting body may prepare and provide an assessment to a reporting entity regarding matches between personal information disclosed by the reporting entity and information held by the credit reporting body. The assessment must evaluate whether the individual's name, residential address, or date of birth matches the credit reporting body's records. The credit reporting body must use this information for the purpose of preparing the assessment. The assessment is taken to be authorised under Australian Privacy Principle 6 for the purposes of paragraph 6.2(b) of the Privacy Act 1988.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for credit reporting bodies to use personal information for identity verification assessments, which may impact cross-border data transfers if the credit reporting body operates internationally. The assessment must be an overall match evaluation without separate category assessments.

requirements.text: A credit reporting body may prepare and provide to a reporting entity an assessment of whether any or all of the individual's name, residential address, or date of birth matches personal information held by the credit reporting body
A credit reporting body must use personal information about the individual and other individuals' names, residential addresses, and dates of birth for the purpose of preparing the assessment
The assessment must be an overall evaluation of the extent of the match between disclosed information and credit reporting body records
The assessment must not include separate assessments of matches between particular categories of personal information

sections.source_quote: A credit reporting body that receives a verification request from a reporting entity in relation to an individual may: (a) prepare and provide to the reporting entity an assessment in accordance with this section of whether any or all of the following personal information matches (in whole or part) personal information held by the credit reporting body: (i) the individual's name; (ii) the individual's residential address; (iii) the individual's date of birth; and (b) use the personal information about the individual, and personal information held by the credit reporting body that is the names, residential addresses and dates of birth of other individuals, for the purpose of preparing the assessment.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting entities' obligation to notify identity verification failures
sections.summary: A reporting entity must provide written notice to an individual if it is unable to verify their identity after receiving an assessment from a credit reporting body. The notice must state the inability to verify identity, specify the credit reporting body that provided the assessment, and offer an alternative means of identity verification. This notification is taken to be authorised under paragraph 20E(3)(e) of the Privacy Act 1988 for the purposes of the assessment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires reporting entities to notify individuals when identity verification fails, which may affect data transfer processes if the credit reporting body is involved in international transactions.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must give a written notice to the individual stating they are unable to verify the individual's identity having regard to the assessment
The notice must specify the name of the credit reporting body that provided the assessment
The notice must offer the individual an alternative means of verifying their identity

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must give a written notice to the individual: (a) stating that the reporting entity is unable to verify the identity of the individual having regard to the assessment; and (b) specifying the name of the credit reporting body that provided the assessment; and (c) offering the individual an alternative means of verifying the identity of the individual.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Retention of verification information for 7 years
sections.summary: Credit reporting bodies must retain specific information related to verification requests for 7 years after the request is received. This includes the name of the reporting entity, the date of the request, the personal information provided by the reporting entity, and the date the assessment was provided. After the 7-year period, credit reporting bodies must delete this information. This section also applies to reporting entities, which must retain records of verification requests for 7 years from the request date, provided they do not provide designated services to the individual during that period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates 7-year retention of verification information, which impacts data storage practices and cross-border data transfers if the information is held internationally.

requirements.text: A credit reporting body must retain the name of the reporting entity, date of request, personal information provided by the reporting entity, and date of assessment for 7 years after the request was received
A credit reporting body must delete the information at the end of the 7-year period
A reporting entity must make a record of the credit reporting body, personal information provided, and assessment (if any) for 7 years from the request date
A reporting entity must delete the record at the end of the 7-year period after the request was made

sections.source_quote: A credit reporting body that receives a verification request in relation to an individual must retain the following information for 7 years after the request was received: (a) the name of the reporting entity that made the request; (b) the date on which the request was made; (c) the personal information about the individual that was provided by the reporting entity to the credit reporting body; (d) the date on which the credit reporting body provided an assessment (if any); (e) such other information about the verification request as is

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Access to verification information for individuals
sections.summary: Individuals have the right to access verification information held by credit reporting bodies or reporting entities that relates to a verification request or assessment. Credit reporting bodies and reporting entities must take reasonable steps to ensure individuals can obtain access to this information. This right applies to personal information that relates to verification requests or assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants individuals access to verification information, which may impact data transfer processes if the information is held internationally.

requirements.text: A credit reporting body or reporting entity in possession of verification information must take reasonable steps to ensure the individual can obtain access to the information

sections.source_quote: A credit reporting body or a reporting entity in possession or control of personal information, or other information of a kind referred to in subsection 35E(1), that relates to a verification request or an assessment in relation to an individual must take reasonable steps to ensure that the individual can obtain access to the information.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Unauthorised access to verification information
sections.summary: Unauthorized access to verification information that relates to a verification request or assessment constitutes an offence under this section. The penalty is 300 penalty units. However, this offence does not apply if the access is obtained in accordance with or as authorized by this Act or any other law. This section addresses security breaches related to verification information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines penalties for unauthorized access to verification information, which is relevant for data security assessments in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Unauthorized access to verification information that relates to a verification request or assessment is an offence punishable by 300 penalty units

sections.source_quote: A person who obtains access to information that is personal information relating to a verification request or an assessment in relation to an individual is guilty of an offence punishable by 300 penalty units.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Pre-commencement customers definition
sections.summary: A pre-commencement customer is defined as a customer of a reporting entity that commenced services before 12 December 2007 or whose business relationship involved only designated services covered by specific tables in section 6 of the Act as of 1 July 2026. Pre-commencement customers cease to be pre-commencement customers when the reporting entity complies with subsection 28(1) in relation to the customer. This definition applies to customers in specific business relationships.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines pre-commencement customers, which may impact data transfer processes for customers with historical business relationships.

requirements.text: A customer is a pre-commencement customer if the reporting entity commenced before 12 December 2007 to provide a designated service covered by an item of table 1, 2 or 3 in section 6 of this Act to the customer
A customer is a pre-commencement customer if the business relationship involved only designated services covered by specific tables in section 6 as of 1 July 2026
A pre-commencement customer ceases to be a pre-commencement customer when the reporting entity complies with subsection 28(1) in relation to the customer

sections.source_quote: A customer of a reporting entity is a pre-commencement customer if: (a) the reporting entity commenced before 12 December 2007 to provide a designated service covered by an item of table 1, 2 or 3 in section 6 of this Act to the customer; or (b) the business relationship between the reporting entity and the customer involved the provision of only any of the following designated services in section 6 of this Act as at the start of 1 July 2026: (i) designated services covered by item 2 of table 2 in section 6; (ii) designated services covered by table 5 in section 6; (iii) designated services covered by table 6 in section 6.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Agent authorization for KYC compliance
sections.summary: Reporting entities may authorize agents to comply with KYC requirements under paragraphs 28(3)(c) and (d). The reporting entity remains liable for contraventions even when using agents. Agents can be authorized directly or through other persons to handle customer KYC information. Agency principles apply without limiting other agency operations under this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows third-party agents to handle KYC data for financial services, requiring reporting entities to maintain liability for compliance with KYC rules when using agents.

requirements.text: Reporting entity may authorize an agent for KYC compliance with paragraphs 28(3)(c) and (d)
Reporting entity remains liable for contraventions when using agents
Agent authorization can be direct or through other persons

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity (and not its agent) will be liable to civil penalties for contraventions of this Part for providing designated services to its customers without complying with paragraphs 28(3)(c) and (d) in respect of its customers.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reliance on KYC procedures through agreements
sections.summary: A reporting entity may rely on another party's compliance with KYC requirements if they have reasonable grounds to believe the AML/CTF Rules are satisfied. The entity must have entered a written agreement with the other party and meet specific conditions including obtaining customer identity information. This allows the entity to treat the other party's compliance as their own for designated services.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows financial institutions to use third parties for KYC verification if they meet specific conditions, reducing the need for direct compliance with KYC rules.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must enter written agreement with another person for KYC procedures
Reporting entity must have reasonable grounds to believe AML/CTF Rules requirements are met
Reporting entity must obtain customer identity information from the other party
Reporting entity must have reasonable grounds to believe reliance is appropriate given risk

sections.source_quote: a reporting entity (the first entity) enters into a written agreement or arrangement with another person relating to the first entity's reliance on the collection and verification of KYC information relating to a customer in accordance with paragraphs 28(3)(c) and (d)

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Regular assessments for KYC agreements
sections.summary: Reporting entities must conduct regular assessments of agreements with third parties for KYC compliance. Assessments must follow AML/CTF Rules, be completed at specified intervals, and be documented within 10 business days. This ensures ongoing compliance with KYC requirements when using third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular third-party KYC assessments to maintain compliance, critical for financial institutions using agents or arrangements for customer verification.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must carry out assessments in accordance with AML/CTF Rules
Reporting entity must carry out assessments at times specified by AML/CTF Rules
Reporting entity must prepare written record of each assessment within 10 business days

sections.source_quote: the reporting entity must: carry out assessments in accordance with the AML/CTF Rules; carry out those assessments at the times worked out in accordance with the AML/CTF Rules; and prepare a written record of each assessment within 10 business days after the day of completing the assessment.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for certain designated services
sections.summary: This section provides exemptions for specific designated services including pension transactions, superannuation contributions, and RSA contributions. The exemptions apply to services covered by specific items in table 1 of section 6, allowing reporting entities to avoid compliance with certain parts of the AML/CTF rules for these services.
sections.dtia_summary: Exempts specific financial services like pension and superannuation transactions from KYC requirements, reducing the need for data transfers and verifications for these services.

requirements.text: This Part does not apply to designated services of a kind specified in table 1 of section 6

sections.source_quote: This Part does not apply to a designated service that is of a kind covered by item 40, 42 or 44 of table 1 in section 6

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemption for criminal investigations
sections.summary: Reporting entities may exempt themselves from certain obligations when assisting criminal investigations. This includes not being required to comply with KYC rules if they reasonably believe compliance would alert the customer to an ongoing criminal investigation. The exemption applies to designated services provided to customers during keep open notices.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows financial institutions to bypass KYC checks when assisting with criminal investigations, critical for maintaining customer confidentiality during investigations.

requirements.text: Reporting entity may exempt from section 28, 30 or 26G if compliance would alert customer to criminal investigation

sections.source_quote: section 28, 30 or 26G does not apply to the reporting entity in respect of the provision of a designated service to the customer to the extent that the reporting entity reasonably believes that compliance with that section would or could reasonably be expected to alert the customer to the existence of a criminal investigation.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Keep open notices for criminal investigations
sections.summary: Keep open notices allow agencies to request financial institutions to continue providing services to customers during criminal investigations. These notices are issued by senior agency members who reasonably believe the service would assist in investigating serious offenses. The notices must specify the type of offense and the agency issuing them.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for financial institutions to continue services during criminal investigations without triggering KYC checks, critical for maintaining customer relationships during investigations.

requirements.text: Senior agency member may issue keep open notice if service would assist in serious offense investigation
Keep open notice must be issued for serious offenses punishable by imprisonment for 2 years or more

sections.source_quote: A senior member of an agency may issue a notice (a keep open notice) to a reporting entity if the senior member reasonably believes that the provision of a designated service by the reporting entity to a customer would assist in the investigation by the agency of a serious offence.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Keep open notices: extension and duration rules
sections.summary: A keep open notice remains in force for up to 6 months from the notice date, unless extended. Extensions are permitted for up to 6 months if the agency reasonably believes continued service would assist in investigating serious offences. The notice may be extended only once per extension period. The extension notice must be issued before the original notice expires and the agency must have a valid reason related to serious offence investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for extending keep open notices and their duration, relevant for assessing when cross-border data transfers require extended oversight periods during investigations.

requirements.text: A keep open notice remains in force for 6 months from the notice date or until the investigation ends, whichever occurs first.
An extension notice may extend the keep open notice for up to 6 months if issued before expiry and the agency reasonably believes continued service would assist in serious offence investigations.
The keep open notice may be extended only once per extension period.
The extension notice must be issued to the reporting entity before the keep open notice expires.

sections.source_quote: A keep open notice is in force for the period starting on the day specified in the notice and ending on the earlier of: (i) the day 6 months after the notice date; or (ii) the day the agency notifies the reporting entity and AUSTRAC CEO that the investigation has ended.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC oversight of keep open notices
sections.summary: AUSTRAC must receive copies of keep open notices and extension notices from agencies. AUSTRAC can revoke notices if they don't comply with the Act or AML/CTF Rules. AUSTRAC must notify the agency and reporting entity if a notice is revoked.
sections.dtia_summary: AUSTRAC oversight ensures compliance with keep open notices, critical for assessing when cross-border data transfers require regulatory review during investigations.

requirements.text: The senior member of the agency must send a copy of the keep open notice or extension notice to AUSTRAC CEO at the time of issuance.
AUSTRAC may revoke a keep open notice or extension notice if it doesn't comply with the Act or AML/CTF Rules.
AUSTRAC must notify the agency and reporting entity if it revokes a notice.

sections.source_quote: The senior member of the agency must send a copy of the keep open notice or extension notice to the AUSTRAC CEO at the same time the notice is issued to the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemption for suspicious matter reporting
sections.summary: Reporting entities are exempt from certain obligations when they reasonably believe compliance would alert a customer to their suspicion of illegal activity. This exemption applies to specific designated services and does not override other reporting obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides exemptions for when data transfers related to suspicious activity might alert customers, reducing the need for extensive cross-border data sharing during investigations.

requirements.text: Section 28, 30, or 26G does not apply to the reporting entity if compliance would reasonably alert the customer to the reporting entity's suspicion.

sections.source_quote: Section 28 (undertaking initial customer due diligence) does not apply to the reporting entity in respect of the provision of a designated service to the customer to the extent that the reporting entity reasonably believes that compliance with that section would or could reasonably be expected to alert the customer to the reporting entity's suspicion.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for specific financial services
sections.summary: Reporting entities are exempt from enhanced due diligence for specific financial services if they meet certain conditions, such as the service being a designated service with specific criteria like low-value transactions or superannuation fund withdrawals.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines exemptions for low-value transactions and specific financial services, relevant for assessing when cross-border data transfers can be exempted from strict oversight.

requirements.text: The designated service must be specified in column 1 of the table and meet the conditions in column 2.
The reporting entity does not have an enhanced due diligence obligation under section 32.

sections.source_quote: Section 28 (undertaking initial customer due diligence) does not apply to a reporting entity in respect of the provision of a designated service to a customer if: (a) the designated service is specified in column 1 of an item of the following table; and (b) the conditions (if any) specified in column 2 of the item are satisfied; and (c) the reporting entity does not have an enhanced due diligence obligation in relation to the customer under section 32.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Threshold conditions for reporting small currency transactions
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which reporting entities must report small currency transactions involving physical cash. Reporting entities must report transactions where the account provider is a financial institution or similar entity, the transaction involves physical currency, and the value is less than $1,000. Separate thresholds apply for bullion (less than $5,000) and casino-related transactions involving gaming chips or tokens (less than $5,000 or $5,000 or more).
sections.dtia_summary: These thresholds define when physical cash transactions must be reported to AUSTRAC, impacting data transfer assessments for cross-border cash movements and financial institutions handling small physical currency transactions.

requirements.text: Currency is not moved into or out of an account where the account provider is an ADI, building society, bank, credit union or representative office of a foreign bank
Proceeds are in physical currency form
Funding source is in physical currency form
Value of currency is less than $1,000
Retail value of bullion is less than $5,000
Reporting entity is a casino
Designated service does not involve online gambling services
Designated service involves customer giving or receiving only gaming chips or tokens
Designated service involves an amount of $5,000 or more
Reporting entity providing designated service is an on-course bookmaker or totalisator agency board
Designated service involves an amount of less than $5,000

sections.source_quote: 142 Compilation: Item 10: currency is not moved into or out of an account where the account provider is an ADI, a building society, a bank, a credit union or a representative office of a foreign bank; and either or both of the following apply: (i) proceeds are in physical currency; (ii) funding source is in physical currency; and value of currency is less than $1,000

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemption for intermediary institutions in reporting
sections.summary: This section exempts intermediary institutions from certain reporting obligations when they pass on transfer messages in value chains. Reporting entities must monitor customers for unusual transactions when providing services through permanent establishments in Australia to identify suspicious activity. The exemption applies to transactions covered by item 31 of table 1 in section 6.
sections.dtia_summary: This exemption allows intermediary institutions to avoid certain reporting obligations for specific transactions, but requires monitoring for suspicious activity, which affects data transfer assessments for cross-border financial messaging.

requirements.text: Divisions 1 to 6 do not apply to a designated service covered by item 31 of table 1 in section 6
Reporting entity must monitor customers to identify unusual transactions and behaviors that may give rise to a suspicious matter reporting obligation

sections.source_quote: 39F Exemption—intermediary institutions: Divisions 1 to 6 do not apply to a designated service covered by item 31 of table 1 in section 6. A reporting entity must monitor its customers... to identify unusual transactions and behaviours of the customers (within the meaning of section 30) that may give rise to a suspicious matter reporting obligation.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Simplified reporting obligations for suspicious matters
sections.summary: This section outlines simplified reporting obligations for reporting entities to AUSTRAC. Reporting entities must report suspicious matters, threshold transactions, international value transfers, and virtual asset transfers to unverified self-hosted wallets. Reporting entities may also be required to provide AML/CTF compliance reports to AUSTRAC.
sections.dtia_summary: These simplified reporting obligations define when financial institutions must report specific cross-border transactions to AUSTRAC, impacting data transfer assessments for international financial flows and virtual asset transfers.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO reports about suspicious matters
If a designated service involves a threshold transaction, reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO a report about the transaction
If a person provides an international value transfer service, the person must give AUSTRAC CEO a report about the service
If a person provides a designated service involving transfer of virtual assets to or from unverified self-hosted virtual asset wallet, must give AUSTRAC CEO report about the service
A reporting entity may be required to give AML/CTF compliance reports to AUSTRAC CEO

sections.source_quote: 40 Simplified outline: A reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO reports about suspicious matters. If a designated service involves a threshold transaction, reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO a report about the transaction.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Suspicious matter reporting obligation conditions
sections.summary: This section defines when a suspicious matter reporting obligation arises for reporting entities. The obligation triggers when a reporting entity commences a service with a person, the person requests a service the entity ordinarily provides, or the entity inquires about service provision. The obligation also arises if the reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that the person is not who they claim to be, an agent is not who they claim to be, or information is relevant to investigations of crimes, money laundering, terrorism financing, or proceeds of crime.
sections.dtia_summary: These conditions determine when financial institutions must report suspicious activity to AUSTRAC, directly impacting data transfer impact assessments for cross-border transactions involving potential illicit activity.

requirements.text: Reporting entity commences to provide or proposes to provide a designated service to a person
Person requests reporting entity to provide a designated service the entity ordinarily provides
Person inquires whether reporting entity would provide designated service
Reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds person is not who they claim to be
Reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds agent is not who they claim to be
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of evasion of taxation law
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of evasion of state or territory taxation law
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of Commonwealth or state/territory offences
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to enforcement of Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to enforcement of state/territory law corresponding to Proceeds of Crime Act 2.02
Reporting entity suspects provision of service is preparatory to commission of terrorism financing offence
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of terrorism financing offence
Reporting entity suspects provision of service is preparatory to commission of money laundering offence
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of money laundering offence

sections.source_quote: 41 Reports of suspicious matters: A suspicious matter reporting obligation arises for a reporting entity in relation to a person if... the reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that the person is not the person the person claims to be; or the reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that an agent of the person is not the person the agent claims to be; or the reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that information... may be relevant to investigation of, or prosecution of a person for, an evasion of a taxation law.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Suspicious matter reporting conditions for financial crimes
sections.summary: This section specifies additional conditions under which a suspicious matter reporting obligation arises. The obligation triggers if the reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that information is relevant to investigations of tax evasion, state/territory tax evasion, Commonwealth or state/territory offences, enforcement of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, or money laundering offences. These conditions are part of the broader framework for identifying suspicious activity related to financial crimes.
sections.dtia_summary: These conditions define specific financial crime scenarios that require reporting to AUSTRAC, directly impacting data transfer impact assessments for cross-border transactions involving potential money laundering or terrorism financing activities.

requirements.text: Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of evasion of taxation law
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of evasion of state or territory taxation law
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of Commonwealth or state/territory offences
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to enforcement of Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to enforcement of state/territory law corresponding to Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
Reporting entity suspects provision of service is preparatory to commission of terrorism financing offence
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of terrorism financing offence
Reporting entity suspects provision of service is preparatory to commission of money laundering offence
Reporting entity suspects information is relevant to investigation of money laundering offence

sections.source_quote: 41 Reports of suspicious matters: Reporting entity suspects on reasonable grounds that information... may be relevant to investigation of, or prosecution of a person for, an evasion of a taxation law; or evasion of state or territory taxation law; or Commonwealth or state/territory offences; or enforcement of Proceeds of Crime Act 2002; or money laundering offences.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting timelines for suspicious matters
sections.summary: This section specifies the reporting timelines for suspicious matters. If the suspicion relates to identity fraud, agent fraud, or information relevant to tax evasion, the reporting entity must submit the report within 3 business days of forming the suspicion. If the suspicion relates to terrorism financing or money laundering, the reporting entity must submit the report within 24 hours of forming the suspicion. The report must include specific information as specified in the AML/CTF Rules and a statement of the grounds for the suspicion.
sections.dtia_summary: These strict reporting timelines for suspicious activity directly impact data transfer impact assessments for cross-border transactions, requiring timely reporting of potential financial crimes to AUSTRAC.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must submit report within 3 business days of forming suspicion for identity, agent fraud, or tax evasion related suspicions
Reporting entity must submit report within 24 hours of forming suspicion for terrorism financing or money laundering related suspicions
Report must contain information specified in AML/CTF Rules
Report must contain statement of grounds for suspicion

sections.source_quote: 42 Reporting timelines: If paragraph (1)(d), (e), (f), (i) or (j) applies—3 business days after forming suspicion; if paragraph (1)(g) or (h) applies—24 hours after forming suspicion.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for designated services under AML/CTF Rules
sections.summary: This section outlines exemptions where certain designated services are excluded from the Division. The exemptions are based on the type of service specified in the AML/CTF Rules, the circumstances under which the service is provided, and specific conditions related to foreign permanent establishments. These exemptions apply to reporting entities engaged in designated services that meet the criteria outlined in the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions for services covered by AML/CTF Rules may affect data transfer assessments when services involve cross-border transactions or virtual asset transfers.

requirements.text: This Division does not apply to a designated service that is of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules
This Division does not apply to a designated service that is provided in circumstances specified in the AML/CTF Rules

This Division does not apply to a designated service that is provided by a reporting entity at or through a permanent establishment of the entity in a foreign country, other than a service covered by item 32A of table 1 in section 6

sections.source_quote: This Division does not apply to a designated service that is of a kind specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting threshold transactions to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: Reporting entities must submit reports to AUSTRAC CEO within 10 business days of a threshold transaction. The transaction must involve a designated service and meet specific criteria. This section applies to reporting entities that provide services involving threshold transactions, ensuring timely reporting of transactions that meet the threshold amount.
sections.dtia_summary: Threshold transactions require reporting to AUSTRAC, impacting data transfer assessments for cross-border financial transactions.

requirements.text: The reporting entity must, within 10 business days after the day on which the transaction takes place, give the AUSTRAC CEO a report of the transaction

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must, within 10 business days after the day on which the transaction takes place, give the AUSTRAC CEO a report of the transaction.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for threshold transactions involving ADIs
sections.summary: This section specifies exemptions for threshold transactions where the reporting entity and customer are both Australian Deposit Institutions (ADIs) or hold exchange settlement accounts. These exemptions allow certain transactions to bypass reporting requirements under the threshold transactions framework, provided specific conditions are met.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions for ADIs and exchange settlement accounts may reduce reporting obligations for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: Section 43 does not apply to a designated service that is: (a) provided by a reporting entity that is an ADI; and (b) provided to a customer that is an ADI

Section 43 does not apply to a designated service that is: (a) provided by a reporting entity that is the holder of an exchange settlement account; and (b) provided to a customer that is the holder of an exchange settlement account; and (c) provided using the exchange settlement accounts held by the reporting entity and the customer

sections.source_quote: Section 43 does not apply to a designated service that is: (a) provided by a reporting entity that is an ADI; and (b) provided to a customer that is an ADI.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: International value transfer services definition
sections.summary: This section defines international value transfer services as those that transfer value between Australia and a foreign country. The service must be covered by specific items in the AML/CTF Rules and involve value transfers that cross national borders. This definition is critical for determining which transactions require reporting under the international value transfer framework.
sections.dtia_summary: International value transfer services require specific reporting obligations, impacting data transfer assessments for cross-border financial transactions.

requirements.text: A service is an international value transfer service if: (a) the service is covered by item 29 or 30 of table 1 in section 6; and (b) either: (i) the value to be transferred is in Australia and, as a result of the provision of the service, the value will be in a foreign country; or (ii) the value to be transferred is in a foreign country and, as a result of the provision of the service, the value will be in Australia

sections.source_quote: A service is an international value transfer service if: (a) the service is covered by item 29 or 30 of table 1 in section 6; and (b) either: (i) the value to be transferred is in Australia and, as a result of the provision of the service, the value will be in a foreign country; or (ii) the value to be transferred is in a foreign country and, as a result of the provision of the service, the value will be in Australia.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting international value transfers with deadlines
sections.summary: Reporting entities must submit reports to AUSTRAC within 10 business days of completing international value transfers. The report must include details of the transaction, with exceptions if the transfer is deemed unlikely to occur. This section also specifies that intermediary institutions may discharge reporting obligations under certain conditions, such as when they provide designated services covered by specific items in the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: International value transfers require timely reporting to AUSTRAC, with specific conditions for intermediary institutions to fulfill obligations.

requirements.text: The reporting entity must give the AUSTRAC CEO a report about the provision of the international value transfer service within 10 business days after the reporting entity passes on or receives the transfer message for the transfer of value

Subsection (2) does not apply if, within the 10 business day period mentioned in that subsection, the reporting entity: (a) reasonably determines that the transfer of value will not occur; and (b) takes reasonable steps to ensure that the transfer of value will not occur

The obligation imposed on a reporting entity by subsection (2) may be discharged by an intermediary institution in the value transfer chain if: (a) the intermediary institution is an intermediary institution that provides, or will provide, the designated service covered by item 31 of table 1 in section 6; (b) the reporting entity has entered into a written agreement or arrangement with the intermediary institution; and (c) the written agreement or arrangement enables the intermediary institution to comply with the obligation

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must give the AUSTRAC CEO a report about the provision of the international value transfer service within 10 business days after the reporting entity passes on or receives the transfer message for the transfer of value.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting transfers involving unverified self-hosted virtual asset wallets
sections.summary: This section requires reporting of transfers involving unverified self-hosted virtual asset wallets. The reporting entity must submit details of transactions where virtual assets are received or transferred to a self-hosted wallet that has not been verified by the entity. This applies to specific types of services covered by the AML/CTF Rules and ensures compliance with virtual asset transfer regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers involving unverified self-hosted virtual asset wallets require reporting, impacting data transfer assessments for virtual asset transactions.

requirements.text: The reporting entity must report a designated service covered by item 29 or 30 of table 1 in section 6 that involves: (a) receiving virtual assets transferred from a self-hosted virtual asset wallet; or (b) transferring virtual assets to a self-hosted virtual asset wallet; and (c) the person who controls the self-hosted virtual asset wallet has not been verified by the reporting entity in accordance with the reporting entity's AML/CTF program

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity commences to provide a designated service covered by item 29 or 3 as table 1 in section 6 at or in Australia; and the service involves: (i) receiving virtual assets transferred from a self-hosted virtual asset wallet; or (ii) transferring virtual assets to a self-hosted virtual asset wallet; and the person who controls the self-hosted virtual asset wallet has not been verified by the reporting entity in accordance with the reporting entity's AML/CTF program.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting on designated service commencement
sections.summary: A reporting entity must submit a report to AUSTRAC CEO within 10 business days of starting to provide a designated service. The report details the service provision activities. This obligation applies to entities subject to the AML/CTF Rules but excludes transfers of value specified in the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting on service commencement for DTIA purposes; relevant for tracking when data transfer mechanisms begin operations.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO a report about provision of designated service within 10 business days after commencing service

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must give the AUSTRAC CEO a report about the provision of the designated service within 10 business days after commencing to provide the service.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF compliance reporting scope
sections.summary: Reporting entities must submit compliance reports to AUSTRAC CEO within the lodgment period for a reporting period. The reports cover adherence to the Act, regulations, and AML/CTF Rules during that period. This applies to entities with specified reporting periods and lodgment periods, excluding those fully covered by other reporting mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines reporting timelines and scope for compliance reports; critical for DTIA to determine reporting obligations when transferring financial data.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO a report relating to compliance with Act, regulations and AML/CTF Rules during reporting period within lodgment period

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must, within the lodgment period for a reporting period, give the AUSTRAC CEO a report relating to the reporting entity's compliance with this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules during the reporting period.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Self-incrimination exemption for reports
sections.summary: A person cannot claim self-incrimination as a defense when providing reports under section 47. The report may be used in proceedings for contraventions of section 47(2), offenses under section 136, or section 137.1 of the Criminal Code. This ensures full cooperation with AUSTRAC reporting requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies that reporting entities cannot avoid obligations due to self-incrimination concerns; relevant for DTIA to ensure compliance with reporting requirements.

requirements.text: A person is not excused from giving a report under section 47 on the ground that the report might tend to incriminate the person

sections.source_quote: A person is not excused from giving a report under section 47 on the ground that the report might tend to incriminate the person or expose the person to a penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Amending or withdrawing reports
sections.summary: AML/CTF Rules may specify procedures for amending or withdrawing reports submitted under sections 41, 43, 46, or 46A. This includes timeframes for requests, amendment processes, and consequences of amendments. The rules may address how reports are modified or withdrawn after submission.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for report corrections or withdrawals; important for DTIA to assess how data transfers can be adjusted post-implementation.

requirements.text: AML/CTF Rules may make provision in relation to amendment or withdrawal of reports given under sections 41, 43, 46 or 46A

sections.source_quote: The AML/CTF Rules may make provision in relation to the amendment or withdrawal of a report given under section 41, 43, 46 or 46A.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Requesting additional information from reporting entities
sections.summary: AUSTRAC CEO may require reporting entities to provide additional information or documents within specified timeframes. The notice must be issued only if the recipient has knowledge or possession of the information. The 14-day minimum timeframe applies unless the issuer considers a shorter period is reasonable and necessary.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows AUSTRAC to request additional data from entities; relevant for DTIA to assess data sharing requirements when transferring financial information.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC CEO may require reporting entity to provide further information within specified period
Issuer must reasonably believe recipient has knowledge or possession of specified information
Period for providing information must be at least 14 days unless issuer considers shorter period is reasonable

sections.source_quote: may, by written notice given to the reporting entity or any other person, require the reporting entity or other person: to give such further information as is specified in the notice, within the period and in the manner specified in the notice

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF reporting for remittance affiliates
sections.summary: AML/CTF Rules may specify reporting obligations for registered remittance affiliates within remittance networks. These rules may allow remittance network providers to fulfill obligations of affiliates or accept reports from providers instead of affiliates. This ensures consistent reporting across remittance channels.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses reporting for remittance services; critical for DTIA when assessing cross-border financial data transfers involving remittance networks.

requirements.text: AML/CTF Rules may make provision for reports required by registered remittance affiliates

sections.source_quote: The AML/CTF Rules may make provision for and in relation to reports required by this Part to be given by a reporting entity that is a registered remittance affiliate of a registered remittance network provider.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Compiling information for money laundering investigations
sections.summary: This section requires persons to provide information or documents to AUSTRAC CEO within specified timeframes when the information relates to money laundering, terrorism financing, proliferation financing, or other serious crimes. The notice must specify the information or documents required and the timeframe for compliance. The AUSTRAC CEO must reasonably believe the person has knowledge or control of the specified information or documents before issuing a notice. The timeframe for compliance must be at least 14 days unless the AUSTRAC CEO considers a shorter period is necessary and reasonable.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for sharing financial information with AUSTRAC during money laundering investigations, impacting data transfer to Australian authorities for cross-border financial crime monitoring.

requirements.text: Provide information to AUSTRAC CEO within period and manner specified in notice
Produce documents to AUSTRAC CEO within period and manner specified in notice
AUSTRAC CEO must reasonably believe person has knowledge or control of specified information/documents
Compliance timeframe must be at least 14 days unless shorter period is considered necessary and reasonable

sections.source_quote: 161 (b) identifying trends, patterns, threats or vulnerabilities associated with money laundering, terrorism financing, proliferation financing or other serious crimes; for the purposes of the performance of functions of the AUSTRAC CEO. (3) The AUSTRAC CEO must not give a notice under subsection (2) to a person unless the AUSTRAC CEO reasonably believes that the person has knowledge of the information, or possession or control of the document, that is specified in the notice.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorised disclosure of financial information to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: This section authorises AUSTRAC CEO to require persons to provide information or documents related to money laundering, terrorism financing, proliferation financing, or other serious crimes. The notice must specify the effect of subsection (5) which relates to civil penalties. The person must comply with the notice, and the notice is a civil penalty provision.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides the legal basis for AUSTRAC to obtain financial information from entities for anti-money laundering purposes, affecting data transfer to Australian authorities.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC CEO may authorise person to give information to AUSTRAC CEO
AUSTRAC CEO may authorise person to produce documents to AUSTRAC CEO
Compliance with notice is a civil penalty provision

sections.source_quote: 49C (1) This section applies if a person has information or a document that may assist the AUSTRAC CEO with: (a) obtaining or analysing information to support efforts to combat money laundering, terrorism financing, proliferation financing or other serious crimes; or (b) identifying trends, patterns, threats or vulnerabilities associated with money laundering, terrorism financing, proliferation financing or other serious crimes; for the purposes of the performance of functions of the AUSTRAC CEO.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting foreign card holder information to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: This section requires reporting entities to provide information about foreign credit or debit card holders to AUSTRAC when the card issuer is outside Australia and the reporting entity lacks that information. AUSTRAC CEO or Commissioner of Taxation may direct reporting entities to give card issuers a request for information. Reporting entities must comply with the direction within 10 business days.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific data sharing with AUSTRAC for foreign card transactions, impacting cross-border financial data transfers for AML compliance.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must comply with AUSTRAC CEO or Commissioner of Taxation direction within 10 business days
Reporting entity must give AUSTRAC CEO report about card issuer's response within 20 business days
Reporting entity must give Commissioner of Taxation report about card issuer's response within 20 business days

sections.source_quote: 50 (2) The AUSTRAC CEO or the Commissioner of Taxation may, by written notice given to the reporting entity, direct the reporting entity to give the card issuer a request, in a form specified in the notice, to give the information to the reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting foreign card holder information to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: This section requires reporting entities to provide information about foreign credit or debit card holders to AUSTRAC when the card issuer is outside Australia and the reporting entity lacks that information. AUSTRAC CEO or Commissioner of Taxation may direct reporting entities to give card issuers a request for information. Reporting entities must comply with the direction within 10 business days.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific data sharing with AUSTRAC for foreign card transactions, impacting cross-border financial data transfers for AML compliance.

requirements.text: Reporting entity must comply with direction within 10 business days
Reporting entity must report card issuer's response within 20 business days

sections.source_quote: 164 (3) The reporting entity must comply with the direction within 10 business days after the day on which the direction is given.

sections.heading: Compilation
