sections.title: Secrecy for financial information obtained under AML sections
sections.summary: This section establishes secrecy requirements for information obtained under sections 49, 49B, or 49C. The information must be kept confidential by entrusted investigating officials unless disclosure is for the purposes of their functions, disclosure to other entrusted officials, or disclosure to IGIS officials. The secrecy does not apply when necessary for courts or tribunals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the confidentiality of financial information obtained during AML investigations, affecting data retention and sharing practices across borders.

requirements.text: Person must keep information confidential obtained under sections 49, 49B, or 49C
Confidentiality does not apply if disclosure is for purposes of functions as entrusted investigating official
Confidentiality does not apply if disclosure is to another entrusted investigating official
Confidentiality does not apply if disclosure is to IGIS official for their functions

sections.source_quote: 50A (1) A person is not to be required to produce a document containing information obtained by the person under section 49, 49B or 49C to a court or tribunal; or to disclose information obtained by the person under section 49, 49B or 49C to a court or tribunal.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Legal immunity for AML information disclosures
sections.summary: This section provides legal immunity for persons who communicate or give information under specified AML sections. The person is not considered to have been in possession of the information at any time for the purposes of Division 400 and Chapter 5 of the Criminal Code.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that disclosures of financial information under AML sections do not constitute criminal possession of information for legal purposes.

requirements.text: Person is not considered to have been in possession of information under sections 41, 43, 46, 46A, 49, 49B or 49C for criminal purposes

sections.source_quote: 51 If a person, or an officer, employee or agent of a person, communicates or gives information under section 41, 43, 46, 46A, 49, 49B or 49C, the person, officer, employee or agent is taken, for the purposes of Division 400 and Chapter 5 of the Criminal Code, not to have been in possession of that information at any time.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting entities must be enrolled on the roll
sections.summary: Reporting entities must be entered on the Reporting Entities Roll. If a person starts providing a designated service and isn't already on the roll, they must apply for enrolment within 28 days. This obligation continues until they enrol or cease being a reporting entity. Multiple contraventions are counted per day the deadline passes without action. The roll is maintained electronically by AUSTRAC CEO and can be corrected or removed under specific conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires entities to register for cross-border financial reporting, impacting data transfer assessments by mandating real-time registration of financial entities handling monetary instruments across borders.

requirements.text: Reporting entities must be entered on the Reporting Entities Roll
If a person commences to provide a designated service and their name is not on the roll, they must apply for enrolment within 28 days
The obligation to apply for enrolment continues until the person enrolls or ceases being a reporting entity
A person who fails to apply by the deadline commits a separate contravention for each day until they enrol or cease being a reporting entity
The AUSTRAC CEO must maintain the Reporting Entities Roll electronically

sections.source_quote: Reporting entities must be entered on the Reporting Entities Roll. If a person commences to provide a designated service and their name is not entered on the Reporting Entities Roll, the person must, no later than 28 days after the day on which the person commences to provide the designated service, apply under subsection 51E(1) for enrolment as a reporting entity.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enrolment and removal of reporting entities from roll
sections.summary: When a person applies for enrolment and isn't already on the roll, AUSTRAC CEO must enter their name and enrolment details. A person can request removal of their name and details from the roll if they've ceased providing designated services or are unlikely to provide services in the next financial year. AUSTRAC CEO must consider the request based on whether the person has ceased services, likelihood of future services, and outstanding obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for adding and removing entities from the roll, critical for DTIA as it affects which entities must be registered for cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: If a person applies to AUSTRAC CEO and their name is not on the roll, AUSTRAC CEO must enter their name and enrolment details
A person may request AUSTRAC CEO to remove their name and enrolment details from the roll
AUSTRAC CEO must consider the request based on whether the person has ceased providing designated services, likelihood of future services, and outstanding obligations

sections.source_quote: If a person applies to the AUSTRAC CEO under subsection 51E(1) and the person's name is not already entered on the Reporting Entities Roll, the AUSTRAC CEO must enter on the Reporting Entities Roll: (a) the person's name; and (b) the person's enrolment details.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Cross-border monetary instrument movements require reporting
sections.summary: Cross-border movements of monetary instruments valued at $10,000 or more must be reported to AUSTRAC CEO, a customs officer, or a police officer. This applies to both incoming and outgoing movements of funds across borders, with specific exemptions for commercial carriers and goods carriers.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates reporting of cross-border financial transfers exceeding $10,000, directly impacting DTIA by requiring assessment of reporting mechanisms for international monetary transfers.

requirements.text: Cross-border movements of monetary instruments valued at $10,000 or more must be reported to AUSTRAC CEO, a customs officer, or a police officer

sections.source_quote: Cross-border movements of monetary instruments must be reported to the AUSTRAC CEO, a customs officer or a police officer if the total value moved is $10,000 or more.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting obligations for cross-border monetary movements
sections.summary: Moving monetary instruments into or out of Australia valued at $10,000 or more requires a report to AUSTRAC CEO, a customs officer, or a police officer. This includes both the movement and receipt of funds, with specific exemptions for commercial passenger and goods carriers. Reports must contain specified information and be submitted within 5 business days of receipt.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed reporting of cross-border financial transactions exceeding $10,000, critical for DTIA as it defines the scope and timing for international financial data transfers.

requirements.text: A person must report movements of monetary instruments into or out of Australia valued at $10,000 or more to AUSTRAC CEO, a customs officer, or a police officer
Reports must be given within 5 business days of the receipt of monetary instruments

sections.source_quote: A person must not move one or more monetary instruments into Australia or out of Australia if the sum of the monetary instrument amounts is $10,000 or more and a report in respect of the movement is not given in accordance with this section.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of moving monetary instruments out of Australia
sections.summary: This section defines when a person moves a monetary instrument out of Australia. It specifies that moving occurs if the person takes or sends the instrument out of Australia. It further details that moving happens when a person leaves a place where customs officers examine passports after having the instrument in their baggage or taking it into such a place.

requirements.text: A person moves a monetary instrument out of Australia if the person takes or sends the instrument out of Australia

A person is taken to have moved the instrument out of Australia when leaving a place where customs officers examine passports after having the instrument in their baggage or taking it into such a place

sections.source_quote: 175 (1) For the purposes of this Act, a person moves a monetary instrument out of Australia if the person takes or sends the instrument out of Australia. (2) For the purposes of this Act, if: (a) a person arranges to leave Australia on an aircraft or ship; and (b) either: (i) the person has a monetary instrument in the person's baggage, and the person enters a place at which customs officers examine passports; or (ii) the person takes a monetary instrument into a place at which customs officers examine passports; the person is taken to have moved the instrument out of Australia when the person leaves that place.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Customs officers' obligation to report to AUSTRAC
sections.summary: Customs officers and police officers must forward reports received under sections 53 or 54 to AUSTRAC CEO within 5 business days of receipt. This applies to reports related to reporting obligations under the Act. The requirement is time-bound and specific to the reporting process.
sections.dtia_summary: Customs officers must report cross-border financial transactions to AUSTRAC within 5 business days, impacting data transfer timelines and compliance for financial institutions.

requirements.text: If a customs officer or police officer receives a report under section 53 or 54, the officer must forward it to the AUSTRAC CEO before the end of 5 business days beginning on the day of the receipt

sections.source_quote: If a customs officer or police officer receives a report under section 53 or 54, the officer must forward it to the AUSTRAC CEO before the end of 5 business days beginning on the day of the receipt.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Customs notices for travellers to Australia
sections.summary: The person in charge of an aircraft or ship traveling to Australia must provide a copy of a specified notice to all travelers on board, including crew members. This notice relates to reporting obligations under the Act and must be communicated in a manner prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules. The requirement applies to aircraft or ships that travel directly to Australia without stopping outside.
sections.dtia_summary: Travelers must receive notices about reporting obligations before entering Australia, affecting data transfer procedures for financial transactions.

requirements.text: If an aircraft or ship leaves a place outside Australia to travel to a place in Australia without stopping at any other place outside Australia, the person in charge of the aircraft or ship must give a copy of the notice to all persons traveling on the aircraft or ship (including members of the crew)

sections.source_quote: If an aircraft or ship leaves a place outside Australia to travel to a place in Australia without stopping at any other place outside Australia, the person in charge of the aircraft or ship must: (a) give a copy of the notice, or communicate the information contained in the notice in the manner prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules, to all persons travelling on the aircraft or ship (including members of the crew);

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Simplified outline of institutions in value transfers
sections.summary: This section provides a simplified outline of the obligations for ordering, beneficiary, and intermediary institutions in value transfers. It specifies that each institution type has distinct information collection, verification, and reporting obligations. Additional obligations apply if the transfer involves virtual assets.

requirements.text: An ordering institution, a beneficiary institution and an intermediary institution in a transfer of value must fulfil certain obligations in relation to the transfer
The obligations of an ordering institution relate to the information the institution collects, verifies and provides as part of the transfer
The obligations of a beneficiary institution relate to the information the institution receives or otherwise obtains as part of the transfer
The obligations of an intermediary institution relate to the information the institution receives and provides as part of the transfer
Additional obligations apply if the transfer of value is a transfer of a virtual asset

sections.source_quote: The following is a simplified outline of this Part. • An ordering institution, a beneficiary institution and an intermediary institution in a transfer of value must fulfil certain obligations in relation to the transfer. • The obligations of an ordering institution relate to the information the institution collects, verifies and provides as part of the transfer. • The obligations of a beneficiary institution relate to the information the institution receives or otherwise obtains as part of the transfer. • The obligations of an intermediary institution relate to the information the institution receives and provides as part of the transfer. • Additional obligations apply if the transfer of value is a transfer of a virtual asset.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of institutions in value transfers
sections.summary: This section defines the criteria for determining whether a person is an ordering institution, beneficiary institution, or intermediary institution in a value transfer chain. It specifies that certain persons are excluded from being an ordering institution, beneficiary institution, or intermediary institution, such as those providing value transfers incidentally to another service unless they meet specific conditions.

requirements.text: Whether a person is an ordering institution is to be determined in accordance with the criteria or other requirements specified in the AML/CTF Rules

A person is not an ordering institution if they transfer value in circumstances where the transfer is reasonably incidental to the provision of another service unless they are a financial institution or providing an international value transfer service incidentally to a designated service covered by specific rules

sections.source_quote: Whether a person is an ordering institution is to be determined in (a) criteria or other requirements that a person must satisfy to be an ordering institution; (b) circumstances in which a person is an ordering institution.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions of institutions in value transfers
sections.summary: This section defines the criteria for determining whether a person is an intermediary institution in a value transfer chain. It specifies that a person is an intermediary institution if they receive and pass on transfer messages for value transfers in a chain, or are specified in the AML/CTF Rules. Certain persons are excluded from being an intermediary institution, such as those who solely provide infrastructure for transfer messages.

requirements.text: A person is an intermediary institution if the person in the course of carrying on a business receives and passes on a transfer message for a transfer of value in a value transfer chain

A person is not an intermediary institution if they solely provide the infrastructure that permits persons to send transfer messages for a transfer of value to another person in the value transfer chain

sections.source_quote: A person is an intermediary institution if the person: (a) in the course of carrying on a business, receives and passes on a transfer message for a transfer of value in a value transfer chain; or (b) is specified in the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Same entity for payer and payee in value transfers
sections.summary: This section allows the payer and payee to be the same person in a value transfer. It also permits the ordering institution and beneficiary institution to be the same entity. These provisions define permissible scenarios where institutions may not require separate entities for value transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows same entity for payer/payee and ordering/beneficiary institutions in value transfers, reducing cross-border complexity but not directly impacting DTIA scope.

requirements.text: The payer and the payee in relation to a transfer of value may be the same person
The ordering institution and the beneficiary institution in relation to a transfer of value may be the same person

sections.source_quote: 182 Compilation (a) the payer and the payee in relation to a transfer of value may be the same person; and (b) the ordering institution and the beneficiary institution in relation to a transfer of value may be the same person.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF obligations for ordering institutions
sections.summary: Ordering institutions must collect specified AML/CTF information before processing value transfers. They must verify this information if required by the AML/CTF Rules. If the ordering and beneficiary institutions differ, they must pass the information to the next institution in the value chain. The section also specifies that transfer messages must be processed before the transfer effect occurs.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ordering institutions to collect and verify AML/CTF data before value transfers, with information sharing obligations across the chain.

requirements.text: Before passing on a transfer message for the transfer of value, the ordering institution must collect the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules
If required by the AML/CTF Rules, the ordering institution must verify the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules in accordance with sections 28 and 30
If the ordering institution and beneficiary institution are not the same, the ordering institution must pass the AML/CTF information to the next institution in the value transfer chain
The transfer message must be processed before the ordering institution gives effect to the transfer of value

sections.source_quote: 183 (2) Before the ordering institution passes on a transfer message for the transfer of value, or otherwise gives effect to the transfer of value, the ordering institution must: (a) collect the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules; and (b) if required by the AML/CTF Rules—verify the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules in accordance with sections 28 and 30 (as applicable).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF obligations for beneficiary institutions
sections.summary: Beneficiary institutions must monitor whether they received the required AML/CTF information and whether the payee information is accurate. If they lack complete information or it is inaccurate, they must refuse to release funds or take other actions as defined by their AML/CTF program.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires beneficiary institutions to verify information accuracy and completeness before releasing funds, with refusal options if data is incomplete or inaccurate.

requirements.text: The beneficiary institution must take reasonable steps to monitor whether it has received the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules relating to the transfer of value
The beneficiary institution must take reasonable steps to monitor whether the information received about the payee is accurate
If the beneficiary institution detects incomplete information or inaccuracies, it must refuse to make the transferred value available to the payee
If the beneficiary institution detects incomplete information or inaccuracies, it must take such other action as the beneficiary institution determines

sections.source_quote: 184 (2) The beneficiary institution must take reasonable steps to monitor: (a) whether it has received the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules relating to the transfer of value; and (b) whether the information received about the payee is accurate.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF obligations for intermediary institutions
sections.summary: Intermediary institutions must monitor whether they received the required AML/CTF information. If they lack complete information, they must refuse to pass on the transfer message or take other actions as defined by their AML/CTF program. They must also include relevant information when passing on transfer messages.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires intermediaries to verify information completeness and take action if incomplete, with specific information inclusion requirements for transfers.

requirements.text: The intermediary institution must take reasonable steps to monitor whether it has received the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules relating to the transfer of value
If the intermediary institution detects incomplete information, it must refuse to pass on the transfer message for the transfer of value
If the intermediary institution detects incomplete information, it must take such other action as the intermediary institution determines
The intermediary institution must include information specified in the AML/CTF Rules received from the previous institution in the value transfer chain
The intermediary institution must provide the information referred to in subsection (4) to another institution in the value transfer chain as soon as practicable after receiving a request

sections.source_quote: 185 (2) The intermediary institution must take reasonable steps to monitor whether it has received the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules relating to the transfer of value. (3) If the intermediary institution detects that it has not received all of the information mentioned in subsection (2), and the intermediary institution has not otherwise obtained the information, the intermediary institution must, in accordance with its AML/CTF program, do at least one of the following: (a) refuse to pass on the transfer message for the transfer of value; (b) take such other action as the intermediary institution determines.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF obligations for virtual asset transfers
sections.summary: Ordering institutions must conduct due diligence to determine the type of virtual asset wallet being used for transfers. They must pass on AML/CTF information to beneficiary institutions if they are licensed under FATF Recommendations or not.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires due diligence on virtual asset wallets and information sharing between institutions for virtual asset transfers.

requirements.text: Before commencing to provide the designated service, an ordering institution must undertake due diligence to determine whether the virtual asset wallet is controlled by a licensed person under FATF Recommendations

Before commencing to provide the designated service, an ordering institution must undertake due diligence to determine whether the virtual asset wallet is controlled by a person not required to be licensed under FATF Recommendations

Before commencing to provide the designated service, an ordering institution must undertake due diligence to determine whether the virtual asset wallet is controlled by a person required to be licensed under FATF Recommendations but not so licensed
Before commencing to provide the designated service, an ordering institution must undertake due diligence to determine whether the virtual asset wallet is controlled by the payee

The ordering institution must pass on the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules for the purposes of subsection 64(3) relating to the transfer of value to a beneficiary institution if the beneficiary institution is licensed under FATF Recommendations

The ordering institution must pass on the information specified in the AML/CTF Rules for the purposes of subsection 64(3) relating to the transfer of value to a beneficiary institution if the beneficiary institution is not required to be licensed under FATF Recommendations

sections.source_quote: 187 (2) Before commencing to provide the designated service covered by item 29 of table 1 in section 6, an ordering institution must undertake due diligence to determine, on reasonable grounds, whether the virtual asset wallet to which the virtual asset is being transferred is: (a) a custodial wallet controlled by a person who is licensed or registered under a law that gives effect to the FATF Recommendations; or (b) a custodial wallet controlled by a person who is not required to be licensed or registered under a law that gives effect to the FATF Recommendations; or (c) a custodial wallet controlled by a person who is required to be licensed or registered under a law that gives effect to the FATF Recommendations, but is not so licensed or registered; or (d) a self-hosted wallet controlled by the payee.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Virtual asset transfers: licensing and registration checks for FATF-compliant entities
sections.summary: An ordering institution cannot provide certain virtual asset transfer services if the recipient is required to be licensed under FATF-compliant laws but lacks such licensing. Similarly, beneficiary institutions must conduct due diligence on virtual asset wallets before initiating transfers, verifying whether the wallet is controlled by a licensed entity, an unlicensed entity, or a self-hosted wallet controlled by the payer. Institutions must also ensure they have obtained necessary information before processing transfers, and face penalties for non-compliance with these checks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires verification of recipient licensing status under FATF recommendations for virtual asset transfers, impacting cross-border data flows involving virtual assets and financial institutions.

requirements.text: An ordering institution must not provide the designated service if the recipient is required to be licensed under FATF-compliant laws but is not licensed.
Beneficiary institutions must conduct due diligence to determine if the virtual asset wallet is controlled by a licensed entity, unlicensed entity, or self-hosted wallet controlled by the payer.
Beneficiary institutions must obtain information specified in AML/CTF Rules before processing transfers.
Beneficiary institutions must not provide services if the virtual asset wallet is controlled by a licensed entity but is not licensed.

sections.source_quote: An ordering institution must not provide the designated service covered by item 29 of table 1 in section 6 if this would involve passing on a transfer message for a transfer of value to a person: (a) who is required to be licensed or registered under a law that gives effect to the FATF Recommendations; but (b) is not so licensed or registered.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalties for AML/CTF compliance failures
sections.summary: Subsections (2) to (7) of section 188 are civil penalty provisions. Ordering institutions may exempt certain obligations if they have reasonable grounds that beneficiary institutions cannot securely receive information or safeguard confidentiality, and must maintain records of these exemptions. Beneficiary institutions must also ensure they have appropriate risk management processes before processing transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes civil penalties for non-compliance with virtual asset transfer licensing checks, with exemptions for institutions unable to securely handle information.

requirements.text: Subsections (2) to (7) of section 188 are civil penalty provisions.
Ordering institutions may exempt obligations if they have reasonable grounds that beneficiary institutions cannot securely receive information.
Ordering institutions must maintain records of reasons for not passing on information.
Beneficiary institutions must ensure they have appropriate risk management processes before processing transfers.

sections.source_quote: Subsections (2) to (7) are civil penalty provisions. Ordering institutions may exempt obligations if they have reasonable grounds that beneficiary institutions cannot securely receive information or safeguard confidentiality.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for AML/CTF rules in remittance services
sections.summary: The AML/CTF Rules may exempt specific remittance services or transfers from certain obligations, including for specific types of institutions or circumstances. Exemptions can be tailored to different kinds of institutions, information, or circumstances, but do not override the Acts Interpretation Act 1901.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows exemptions for remittance services under AML/CTF rules, which can impact data transfer compliance for financial institutions.

requirements.text: AML/CTF Rules may provide exemptions for specific remittance services or transfers.
Exemptions can be tailored to different institutions, information, or circumstances.

sections.source_quote: The AML/CTF Rules may provide that this Part, or a specified provision of this Part, does not apply: (a) to a specified kind of designated service; or (b) to a transfer of value that occurs in specified circumstances.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Defence for reliance on third-party information in AML/CTF violations
sections.summary: A person can defend against AML/CTF civil penalty proceedings if they reasonably relied on information provided by another person who was not acting in their capacity as an officer, employee, or agent of the person who relied on the information. This defence applies to contraventions of civil penalty provisions in Division 2.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a defence mechanism for financial institutions that rely on third-party information in AML/CTF compliance, relevant for data transfer assessments.

requirements.text: A person can defend against AML/CTF civil penalty proceedings if they reasonably relied on information from another person.
The other person did not provide information in their capacity as an officer, employee, or agent of the person who relied on the information.

sections.source_quote: In the proceedings, it is a defence if the defendant proves that: (a) the contravention in respect of which the proceedings were instituted was due to reasonable reliance on information given by another person; and (b) the other person did not give the information in the other person's capacity as an officer, employee or agent of the person who relied on the information.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Remittance sector registration framework
sections.summary: This section outlines a tiered registration system for remittance network providers and services, requiring registration for certain activities. Division 2 sets out offences and penalties for unregistered providers, while Division 3 mandates the AUSTRAC CEO to maintain a Remittance Sector Register for registered providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires registration of remittance services and providers, impacting cross-border data flows in the remittance sector.

requirements.text: Providers of registrable remittance network services and services must register with AUSTRAC.

sections.source_quote: This Part provides for a tiered system of registration for providers of registrable remittance network services and providers of registrable remittance services.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Prohibitions on unregistered remittance services
sections.summary: Unregistered persons cannot provide certain remittance services, including remittance network services to non-registered affiliates or independent remittance services without being registered as independent remittance dealers. Affiliates must also be registered with the remittance network provider.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts unregistered entities from providing remittance services across borders, affecting data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: A person must not provide a registrable remittance network service to another person if they are not registered or the recipient is not a registered affiliate.
A person must not provide a registrable remittance service if they are not registered as an independent remittance dealer.
A person must not provide a registrable remittance service as an affiliate if they are not registered as a remittance affiliate.

sections.source_quote: A person must not provide a registrable remittance network service to another person if: (a) the first person is not a registered remittance network provider; or (b) the first person is a registered remittance network provider, but the person to whom the service is provided is not a registered remittance affiliate of the first person.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Offences for remittance service providers
sections.summary: This section defines offences for remittance service providers who fail to comply with requirements under subsections (1), (1A), (1B), or (1C). It specifies that strict liability applies to certain offences involving repeated violations or prior convictions. The penalties include imprisonment for up to 4 years or 1,000 penalty units.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes legal consequences for non-compliance with remittance service requirements, which could impact DTIA by creating obligations for providers to maintain compliance records and avoid repeated violations.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they are subject to a requirement under subsection (1), (1A), (1B) or (1C)
Strict liability applies to paragraphs (2)(b) and (c)
A person commits an offence if they are subject to a requirement under subsection (1), (1A), (1B) or (1C) and the offence relates to subsection (1), (1A), (1B) or (1C) of this section
Strict liability applies to paragraphs (4)(b) and (c)

sections.source_quote: A person commits an offence if: (a) the person is subject to a requirement under subsection (1), (1A), (1B) or (1C); and

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Prohibitions on correspondent banking with shell banks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must not enter correspondent banking relationships with shell banks or financial institutions that have such relationships with shell banks. They must also not permit accounts to be used by shell banks. Additionally, financial institutions must conduct due diligence assessments before and during correspondent banking relationships involving vostro accounts. These obligations apply to financial institutions engaged in correspondent banking.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on cross-border correspondent banking relationships with shell banks, requiring due diligence for vostro accounts and termination procedures if shell banks are involved.

requirements.text: A financial institution must not enter into a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank
A financial institution must not enter into a correspondent banking relationship with another financial institution that has a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank
A financial institution must not enter into a correspondent banking relationship with another financial institution that permits its accounts to be used by a shell bank

A financial institution must carry out due diligence assessments before it enters into, and while it is in, a correspondent banking relationship with another financial institution involving a vostro account

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must not enter into a correspondent banking relationship with: (a) a shell bank; or (b) another financial institution that has a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank; or (c) another financial institution that permits its accounts to be used by a shell bank.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Termination procedures for correspondent banking with shell banks
sections.summary: If a financial institution becomes aware that its correspondent banking relationship involves a shell bank, it must terminate the relationship within 20 days or request the other institution to terminate within 20 days. If the other institution fails to comply within 20 days, the first institution must terminate the relationship within 20 days. These obligations apply to financial institutions with correspondent banking relationships involving shell banks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely termination of correspondent banking relationships with shell banks, with specific timeframes for action and escalation procedures.

requirements.text: If a financial institution becomes aware that the other person is a shell bank, it must terminate the correspondent banking relationship within 20 days after becoming aware or such longer period as the AUSTRAC CEO allows

If a financial institution becomes aware that the other person is a financial institution that has a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank, it must request the other financial institution to terminate the correspondent banking relationship within 20 days after becoming aware or such longer period as the AUSTRAC CEO allows

If the other financial institution fails to comply with a termination request within 20 business days, the first institution must terminate its correspondent banking relationship within 20 days after the end of the first period or such longer period as the AUSTRAC CEO allows

sections.source_quote: If a financial institution (the first institution) is in a correspondent banking relationship with another person and the first institution becomes aware that: (a) the other person is a shell bank; or (b) the other person is a financial institution that has a correspondent banking relationship with a shell bank; or (c) the other person is a financial institution that permits its accounts to be used by a shell bank; the first institution must, within 20 days after becoming so aware or such longer period (if any) as the AUSTRAC CEO allows, do one of the following: (d) terminate the correspondent banking relationship; (e) if paragraph (b) applies—request the other financial institution to terminate the correspondent banking relationship mentioned in that paragraph.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Geographical links for correspondent banking requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions are exempt from correspondent banking requirements if they have a permanent establishment in Australia, are Australian residents with a permanent establishment abroad, or are subsidiaries of Australian companies with permanent establishments abroad. These exemptions apply to financial institutions engaged in correspondent banking relationships.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when financial institutions are exempt from correspondent banking obligations based on geographical presence and residency.

requirements.text: A financial institution is not subject to a requirement under this Part in connection with a correspondent banking relationship unless it carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in Australia

A financial institution is not subject to a requirement under this Part in connection with a correspondent banking relationship unless both: (i) the financial institution is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in a foreign country

A financial institution is not subject to a requirement under this Part in connection with a correspondent banking relationship unless both: (i) the financial institution is a subsidiary of a company that is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in a foreign country

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is not subject to a requirement under this Part in connection with a correspondent banking relationship the financial institution has, or proposes to have, with another person unless: (a) the financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in Australia; or (b) both: (i) the financial institution is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in a foreign country; or (c) both: (i) the financial institution is a subsidiary of a company that is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the financial institution carries on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the financial institution in a foreign country.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Countermeasures for transactions with foreign residents
sections.summary: Regulations may prohibit or regulate transactions with residents of prescribed foreign countries. These prohibitions apply when one party is an Australian resident and the other is a resident of a prescribed foreign country, or when transactions occur through Australian permanent establishments with foreign residents. The regulations may be limited by transaction type, party, or country.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for countermeasures against transactions with foreign residents, which can impact cross-border financial flows and data transfers.

requirements.text: Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party is a resident of Australia and the other party is a resident of a prescribed foreign country

Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment in Australia and the other party is a resident of a prescribed foreign country
Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party is a resident of Australia and the other party is a corporation incorporated in a prescribed foreign country

Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment in Australia and the other party is a corporation incorporated in a prescribed foreign country

Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party is a resident of Australia and the other party is an individual physically present in a prescribed foreign country

Regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where one party enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment in Australia and the other party is an individual physically present in a prescribed foreign country

sections.source_quote: The regulations may prohibit or regulate the entering into of transactions where: (a) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is a resident of a prescribed foreign country; or (b) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the party in Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is a resident of a prescribed foreign country; or (c) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is a corporation incorporated in a prescribed foreign country; or (d) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the party in Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is a corporation incorporated in a prescribed foreign country; or (e) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction is a resident of Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is an individual who is physically present in a prescribed foreign country; or (f) both: (i) one of the parties to the transaction enters into the transaction in the course of carrying on an activity or business at or through a permanent establishment of the party in Australia; and (ii) the other party, or any of the other parties, is an individual who is physically present in a prescribed foreign country.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Record-keeping requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must retain records of customer due diligence, assessments of agreements involving KYC information, AML/CTF programs, and transactions for 7 years. These records apply to reporting entities and must be retained regardless of privacy act provisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates long-term record-keeping for financial institutions, which impacts data storage and retention across borders.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must retain records of customer due diligence for 7 years

A reporting entity must retain records of assessments it carries out of agreements or arrangements it has entered into relating to its reliance on the collection and verification of KYC information about a customer, or other procedures carried out by another person for 7 years
A reporting entity must retain records of its AML/CTF program for 7 years

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must retain records relating to: (a) customer due diligence; and (b) assessments it carries out of agreements or arrangements it has entered into relating to its reliance on the collection and verification of KYC information about a customer, or other procedures, carried out by another person. A reporting entity must retain records relating to its AML/CTF program.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Retention of transaction records for 7 years
sections.summary: Reporting entities must retain sufficient transaction records to reconstruct individual customer transactions for 7 years from the record creation date. Records must comply with AML/CTF Rules. These records are subject to civil penalties if not retained as required. The section specifies that records related to active accounts transferred between Australian Deposit Take-Over (ADI) entities are exempt from retention obligations if transferred within 120 days.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires 7-year retention of transaction records for financial transactions, with specific exemptions for records transferred between ADIs within 120 days. Critical for assessing record retention obligations across borders for financial data.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Records (ATR) Regulator

Records must comply with AML/CTF Rules
Records must be retained for 7 years from creation date
Transferor ADI must give documents to transferee ADI within 120 days before account transfer
Transferor ADI is exempt from retention obligations if documents given to transferee ADI within 120 days

sections.source_quote: If a reporting entity commences to provide, or provides, a designated service to a customer, the reporting entity must retain sufficient records to allow the reconstruction of individual transactions relating to the provision of the designated service to the customer.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Retention of customer-provided transaction documents
sections.summary: Reporting entities must retain customer-provided transaction documents or copies for 7 years from when the customer gives the document. This applies to documents related to active accounts transferred between ADIs within 120 days of transfer. The section specifies that transferee ADIs must retain documents for 7 years if received within the 120-day period.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires 7-year retention of customer documents for financial transactions, with specific timing for transfers between ADIs. Important for assessing record retention across borders for financial data.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Records (ATR) Regulator

requirements.text: Reporting entity must retain customer-provided transaction documents or copies for 7 years
Documents must be retained from the date the customer gives the document

sections.source_quote: The reporting entity must retain: (a) the document; or (b) a copy of the document; for 7 years after the giving of the document.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Retention of records for transferred ADI accounts
sections.summary: When an ADI transfers an active account to another ADI, the transferor must give the relevant documents to the transferee within 120 days before the transfer. The transferor is exempt from retention obligations if documents are transferred within this period. The transferee must retain documents for 7 years if received within the 120-day window.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires 120-day window for transferring records between ADIs, with 7-year retention for transferee ADIs. Critical for assessing record transfer obligations across borders for financial data.
sections.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Records (ATR) Regulator

requirements.text: Transferor ADI must give documents to transferee ADI within 120 days before account transfer
Transferor ADI is exempt from retention obligations if documents given within 120 days
Transferee ADI must retain documents for 7 years if received within 120 days

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Treatment of partnerships as legal persons
sections.summary: This section treats partnerships as persons for legal purposes, imposing obligations on individual partners instead of the partnership. Offences committed by the partnership are attributed to each partner, with exceptions for partners who did not know of contraventions or took reasonable steps to correct them. The section applies to civil penalty provisions similarly to offences.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs involving partnerships, as it defines how obligations and offences are distributed among partners, affecting data handling responsibilities within group structures.

requirements.text: Partnerships are treated as persons for legal purposes with changes specified in this section
Obligations imposed on partnerships are distributed to individual partners

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs involving unincorporated associations, as it defines how obligations and offences are distributed among committee members, affecting data handling responsibilities within group structures.

sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs involving trusts, as it defines how obligations and offences are distributed among trustees, affecting data handling responsibilities within group structures.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Concurrent operation of State and Territory laws
sections.summary: This section states that the Act does not exclude or limit the operation of State or Territory laws that can operate concurrently with the Act. It ensures that State and Territory laws remain in effect alongside the Act without conflict.

sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs involving data collection, as it confirms that the Act does not restrict existing information-gathering powers, which may impact data handling procedures.

requirements.text: The Act does not affect the law relating to legal professional privilege

sections.summary: This section provides exceptions to the tipping off offence for disclosures made in good faith to dissuade customers from illegal activities. The exception requires the disclosure to be made in good faith and for legitimate crime prevention purposes.

sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions for exempting monetary movements from reporting, relevant to DTIAs when assessing whether transactions avoid reporting obligations under AML/CTF frameworks.

requirements.text: The first person must prove they did not conduct or cause movements for the sole or dominant purpose of avoiding reporting under section 53
The exemption applies if the person did not conduct or cause movements for the sole or dominant purpose of ensuring no report is made under section 53

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer may enter any reporting entity business premises with the occupier's consent or under a monitoring warrant.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Audit authority entry powers
sections.summary: This section grants AUSTRAC's CEO the authority to require reporting entities to undergo external audits or money laundering and terrorism financing risk assessments. It specifies that authorised officers may enter business premises with occupier consent or under a monitoring warrant, and may exercise monitoring powers during entry. The section also outlines the CEO's power to mandate external audits and risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes audit powers for financial institutions, relevant to DTIAs when assessing compliance with AML/CTF requirements and audit procedures.

requirements.text: An authorised officer may enter reporting entity business premises with occupier consent
An authorised officer may enter reporting entity business premises under a monitoring warrant
The AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to carry out an external audit or money laundering and terrorism financing risk assessment

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer may enter any reporting entity business premises: (a) with the occupier's consent; or (b) under a monitoring warrant.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorised officer appointment and identity card requirements
sections.summary: This section details the appointment of authorised officers by AUSTRAC CEO, including criteria for appointment and the requirement to issue identity cards. It specifies that authorised officers must comply with AUSTRAC CEO directions and that identity cards must contain a recent photograph and be in an approved form. The section also outlines the process for returning identity cards after ceasing to be an authorised officer.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes identity and appointment protocols for AUSTRAC officers, relevant to DTIAs when assessing internal controls and officer accountability in financial institutions.

AUSTRAC CEO must not appoint a person unless they satisfy conditions specified in regulations
Authorised officers must comply with AUSTRAC CEO directions
AUSTRAC CEO must issue identity cards to authorised officers
Identity cards must be in a form approved by AUSTRAC CEO
Identity cards must contain a recent photograph of the authorised officer

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO may, in writing, appoint as an authorised officer for the purposes of this Act: (a) a member of the staff of AUSTRAC; or (b) a person whose services are made available to the AUSTRAC CEO under subsection 225(3), other than a person covered by paragraph 225(3)(g).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Identity card retention and penalties
sections.summary: This section specifies that authorised officers must carry identity cards when exercising powers, with a penalty of 1 penalty unit for non-compliance. It also outlines the requirement that identity cards must be returned to AUSTRAC CEO within 3 business days after ceasing to be an authorised officer, with a reasonable excuse allowed for non-compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures accountability for AUSTRAC officers through identity card retention, relevant to DTIAs when assessing officer compliance and internal controls.

requirements.text: Authorised officers must carry identity cards at all times when exercising powers
The person must return identity cards to AUSTRAC CEO within 3 business days after ceasing to be an authorised officer
The person may have a reasonable excuse for not returning identity cards within 3 business days

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer must carry the identity card at all times when exercising powers or performing functions as an authorised officer under this Part.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorised officer monitoring powers
sections.summary: This section grants authorised officers the power to enter premises with consent or under a monitoring warrant, and to exercise monitoring powers such as searching for compliance records, systems, reports, and other relevant items. It specifies that officers must leave premises if the occupier asks, and that monitoring powers include the ability to secure items for up to 24 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines monitoring powers for AUSTRAC officers, relevant to DTIAs when assessing physical and digital access controls for financial data.

requirements.text: Authorised officers may enter reporting entity business premises at any reasonable time of the day
Authorised officers may exercise monitoring powers set out in section 148
Authorised officers must leave premises if the occupier asks

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer may enter any reporting entity business premises at any reasonable time of the day.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorised officer monitoring powers details
sections.summary: This section details the specific monitoring powers of authorised officers, including the ability to search premises for compliance records, systems, reports, and other relevant items. It specifies that monitoring powers include the ability to secure items for up to 24 hours if found during the exercise of powers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides detailed monitoring powers for AUSTRAC officers, relevant to DTIAs when assessing physical and digital access controls for financial data.

requirements.text: Authorised officers may search premises for compliance records kept at or accessible from premises
Authorised officers may search premises for systems used by reporting entities for keeping compliance records
Authorised officers may search premises for reports retained at or accessible from premises
Authorised officers may search premises for systems used by reporting entities for preparing, sending, or retaining reports
Authorised officers may search premises for other things relevant to reporting entity obligations
Authorised officers may examine activities conducted on premises relevant to information provided under this Act
Authorised officers may examine things on premises relevant to information provided under this Act
Authorised officers may take photographs, video, audio recordings, or sketches of relevant activities or things
Authorised officers may inspect documents relevant to information provided under this Act
Authorised officers may take extracts or copies of relevant documents
Authorised officers may take equipment and materials required for exercising powers
Authorised officers may secure items for no more than 24 hours if found during exercise of powers

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Act, the following are the monitoring powers that an authorised officer may exercise, in relation to premises, under section 147: (a) the power to search the premises for any compliance records that are kept at, or accessible from, the premises;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Conditions for seizing evidence under monitoring powers
sections.summary: An authorised officer may seize evidence if they reasonably believe it proves an offence under this Act, Crimes Act 1914, or Criminal Code, and it must be secured before a warrant is obtained to prevent concealment, loss, or destruction. The circumstances must be serious and urgent. This applies to evidence relevant to assessing compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Seizure conditions require evidence to be relevant to compliance assessments and must be secured before a warrant to prevent destruction. This affects data retention and evidence handling in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: An authorised officer must believe on reasonable grounds that the thing affords evidence of an offence under this Act, Crimes Act 1914, or Criminal Code
The thing must be necessary to secure before a warrant to prevent concealment, loss, or destruction
The circumstances must be serious and urgent

sections.source_quote: 275 (b) an authorised officer believes on reasonable grounds that: (i) the thing affords evidence of the commission of an offence against this Act or the regulations, or evidence of the commission of an offence against the Crimes Act 1914 or the Criminal Code that relates to this Act or the regulations; and (ii) it is necessary to secure the thing in order to prevent it from being concealed, lost or destroyed before a warrant to seize the thing is obtained; and (iii) the circumstances are serious and urgent.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Tampering with secured items under monitoring powers
sections.summary: Tampering with items secured by an authorised officer under monitoring powers is prohibited. This applies to items that have been secured to prevent concealment, loss, or destruction of evidence. The prohibition covers both physical and digital items relevant to compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Tampering with secured evidence affects data integrity and retention. This section is critical for DTIAs involving evidence handling across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A thing must not be tampered with after being secured by an authorised officer under monitoring powers

sections.source_quote: 276 (a) a thing has been secured by an authorised officer in the exercise of the monitoring powers set out in section 148; and (b) the person tampers or interferes with the thing.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Self-incrimination exemption for compliance questions
sections.summary: A person cannot claim self-incrimination as a defense when answering questions or producing documents related to the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules. This includes civil proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 and criminal proceedings related to sections 136, 137, or 137.1-137.2 of the Criminal Code.
sections.dtia_summary: Self-incrimination exemptions apply to compliance-related data sharing and questioning. This affects data subject rights and disclosure in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: A person is not excused from answering questions or producing documents under subsection (2) on the ground that it might tend to incriminate them

sections.source_quote: 277 (4) A person is not excused from answering a question or producing a document under subsection (2) on the ground that the answering of the question or the production of the document might tend to incriminate the person or expose the person to a penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Identity verification and consent requirements for entries
sections.summary: An authorised officer must produce an identity card upon request before exercising powers. Consent for entry must be informed, voluntary, and limited to a specific period unless withdrawn. The officer must leave premises if consent is withdrawn. This applies to entries related to monitoring powers and compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Identity verification and consent rules impact data access and entry procedures. This is relevant for DTIAs involving physical and digital data access across borders.

requirements.text: An authorised officer must produce identity card for inspection if the occupier requires it
Before obtaining consent, the authorised officer must inform the person they may refuse consent
An entry is not lawful unless the person voluntarily consented to the entry
Consent may be limited to a specific period unless withdrawn before the end of that period
A consent not limited as mentioned in subsection (3) has effect until withdrawn
If consent is withdrawn, the authorised officer must leave the premises

sections.source_quote: 278 (1) Before obtaining the consent of a person for the purposes of paragraph 147(2)(a), the authorised officer must inform the person that he or she may refuse consent. (2) An entry of an authorised officer because of the consent of a person is not lawful unless the person voluntarily consented to the entry.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Announcement and opportunity for entry under monitoring warrants
sections.summary: An authorised officer must announce their authority and provide an opportunity for entry before entering premises under a monitoring warrant. This applies to both occupied premises and those where another person represents the occupier. The officer must identify themselves and provide a copy of the warrant to the occupier or representative.
sections.dtia_summary: Announcement and identification requirements ensure transparency in data access procedures. This affects cross-border data handling and compliance with monitoring powers.

requirements.text: An authorised officer must announce they are authorised to enter the premises
An authorised officer must give any person at the premises an opportunity to allow entry

sections.source_quote: 279 (a) announce that he or she is authorised to enter the premises; and (b) give any person at the premises an opportunity to allow entry to the premises.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Compensation for electronic equipment damage
sections.summary: This section establishes that the Commonwealth must compensate the owner or user of electronic equipment for damage or corruption caused by improper operation. Compensation is determined through mutual agreement between the Commonwealth and the affected party, with a court proceeding available if agreement fails. The amount considers whether the occupier provided appropriate warnings or guidance on equipment operation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses financial liability for electronic equipment damage, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when handling digital assets across borders due to potential data corruption risks.

requirements.text: The Commonwealth must pay reasonable compensation for damage or corruption of electronic equipment
Compensation is determined by mutual agreement between Commonwealth and affected party
If agreement fails, affected party may seek court determination of compensation
Compensation amount considers whether occupier provided appropriate warnings or guidance on equipment operation

sections.source_quote: 281 extension. The occupier is entitled to be heard in relation to that application. The Commonwealth must pay the owner of the equipment, or the user of the data or programs, such reasonable compensation for the damage or corruption as the Commonwealth and the owner or user agree on.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Compensation funding mechanism
sections.summary: This section specifies that compensation for electronic equipment damage must be paid from funds appropriated by Parliament. It establishes the financial mechanism for compensating victims of equipment damage without detailing the specific obligations or conditions for payment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures compensation for electronic equipment damage is funded by parliamentary appropriations, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when handling digital assets across borders.

requirements.text: Compensation for electronic equipment damage is payable out of money appropriated by Parliament

sections.source_quote: 282 Compensation is payable out of money appropriated by the Parliament.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Occupier's rights during warrant execution
sections.summary: This section grants the occupier the right to observe the execution of a monitoring warrant if present at the premises, but this right terminates if the occupier impedes the execution. The section also specifies that multiple areas of premises can be searched simultaneously without affecting the right to observe.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses security monitoring procedures, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when warrants are used for data collection across borders.

requirements.text: Occupier is entitled to observe execution of monitoring warrant if present at premises
Right to observe terminates if occupier impedes execution of warrant
Multiple areas of premises can be searched simultaneously during warrant execution

sections.source_quote: 283 (1) If a monitoring warrant is being executed and the occupier of the warrant premises, or another person who apparently represents the occupier, is present at the premises, the person is entitled to observe the execution of the warrant.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Monitoring warrants for financial entities
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for issuing monitoring warrants, requiring magistrates to be satisfied that it is reasonably necessary for authorized officers to access premises to determine compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing laws. The warrant must specify premises, authorized officers, entry conditions, duration, and purpose.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs monitoring warrants for financial entities, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when conducting cross-border data collection for compliance purposes.

requirements.text: Authorised officer may apply to magistrate for monitoring warrant related to reporting entity business premises
Magistrate may issue warrant if satisfied it is reasonably necessary for authorized officers to access premises for compliance purposes
Magistrate must receive further information from authorized officer before issuing warrant
Warrant must describe premises, authorize authorized officers and assistants, specify entry times, and set expiration date
Warrant must state the purpose for which it is issued

sections.source_quote: 284 (1) An authorised officer may apply to a magistrate for a warrant under this section in relation to reporting entity business premises. sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Magistrates' personal capacity for warrant functions
sections.summary: This section specifies that functions conferred on magistrates by section 159 are held in a personal capacity, not as a court or court member. The magistrate is not required to accept these functions and has the same protection and immunity as if performing the function as a court member.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the legal capacity of magistrates for warrant functions, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when warrants are used for data collection across borders.

requirements.text: Functions conferred on magistrates by section 159 are held in personal capacity

Magistrate performing warrant functions has same protection and immunity as court member

sections.source_quote: 285 (1) The functions conferred on a magistrate by section 159 are conferred on the magistrate: (a) in a personal capacity; and (b) not as a court or a member of a court.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: External audits for financial risk management
sections.summary: This section requires reporting entities to appoint external auditors and conduct audits of their capacity to identify, assess, manage or mitigate money laundering, terrorism financing, and proliferation risks when providing services in Australia. sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates external audits for financial risk management, relevant to data transfer impact assessments when handling cross-border financial data for compliance purposes.

External auditor must conduct audit of reporting entity's capacity to identify, assess, manage or mitigate money laundering, terrorism financing, and proliferation risks
External auditor must provide written report on audit results to reporting entity
Reporting entity must provide AUSTRAC with copy of audit report within specified timeframe

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: External auditor eligibility criteria
sections.summary: This section specifies that an individual cannot be appointed as an external auditor by a reporting entity if they are an officer, employee, or agent of the reporting entity. The section does not impose any obligations on the reporting entity regarding auditor qualifications beyond these exclusions.

sections.source_quote: An individual is not eligible to be appointed an external auditor by a reporting entity if: (a) the individual is an officer, employee or agent of the reporting entity; or (b) both: (i) the reporting entity is a member of a reporting group; and (ii) the individual is an officer, employee or agent of another member of the reporting group.

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: This section establishes that a reporting entity must comply with requirements under section 162 for external audits. It specifies that AUSTRAC CEO can require a reporting entity to appoint an external auditor, arrange for the audit, and provide a written report. The section also states that non-compliance with these requirements is a civil penalty provision.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must comply with a requirement under section 162 for external audits
AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to appoint an external auditor
AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to arrange for the external auditor to carry out an external audit of the reporting entity's compliance with the Act, regulations and AML/CTF Rules
AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to arrange for the external auditor to provide a written report setting out the results of the audit
AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to provide a copy of the audit report to AUSTRAC CEO within a specified period

sections.source_quote: A reporting entity must comply with a requirement under section 162. AUSTRAC CEO may require a reporting entity to appoint an external auditor; and arrange for the external auditor to carry out an external audit of whichever of the following is specified in the notice: (i) the reporting entity's compliance with this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules; (ii) one or more specified aspects of the reporting entity's compliance with this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: External auditor eligibility criteria
sections.summary: This section specifies that an individual cannot be appointed as an external auditor by a reporting entity if they are an officer, employee, or agent of the reporting entity. Additionally, if the reporting entity is part of a reporting group, the individual cannot be an officer, employee, or agent of another group member. The section does not impose any obligations on the reporting entity regarding auditor qualifications beyond these exclusions.

requirements.text: An individual is not eligible to be appointed an external auditor by a reporting entity if they are an officer, employee or agent of the reporting entity

An individual is not eligible to be appointed an external auditor by a reporting entity if the reporting entity is a member of a reporting group and the individual is an officer, employee or agent of another member of the reporting group

sections.source_quote: An individual is not eligible to be appointed an external auditor by a reporting entity if: (a) the individual is an officer, employee or agent of the reporting entity; or (b) both: (i) the reporting entity is a member of a reporting group; and (ii) the individual is an officer, employee or agent of another member of the reporting group.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: External auditor authority and previous audit results
sections.summary: This section allows AUSTRAC CEO to authorize an individual as an external auditor for the purposes of the Act. It also states that an external auditor may consider results of previous audits if the previous audit was completed within the last two years and the auditor is satisfied the previous audit remains relevant. The section does not impose obligations on reporting entities beyond these provisions.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC CEO may authorize a specified individual to be an external auditor for the purposes of this Act
An external auditor may have regard to the results of previous audits if the previous audit was completed within the last two years and the auditor is satisfied the previous audit is still relevant

sections.source_quote: In carrying out an external audit in accordance with a notice under section 161 or 162, an external auditor may, if: (a) an external audit was completed under that section within the last preceding 2 years; and (b) the external auditor is satisfied that the previous audit is still relevant; have regard to the results of the previous audit.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Information-gathering powers for AUSTRAC CEO
sections.summary: This section grants AUSTRAC CEO the power to require a person to produce documents or appear before an examiner for examination if the CEO believes on reasonable grounds that the person has information or documents relevant to compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules. The examiner may record statements made during the examination and those statements are admissible in evidence.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC CEO may require a person to produce documents within the period and manner specified in the notice
AUSTRAC CEO may require a person to appear before an examiner for examination under this Division on oath or affirmation and to answer questions
