sections.source_quote: If the AUSTRAC CEO believes on reasonable grounds that a person has information or a document that is relevant to compliance with this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules, the AUSTRAC CEO may require the person: (a) to produce to the AUSTRAC CEO, within the period and in the manner specified in the notice, any such documents; or (b) to appear before an examiner for examination under this Division on oath or affirmation and to answer questions, and to produce any such documents.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorised officer powers to obtain information and documents
sections.summary: This section allows an authorised officer to require a person to provide information or documents relevant to compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules. The person must comply with the notice within the specified period and manner. Failure to comply with the notice constitutes an offence.

requirements.text: An authorised officer may require a person to provide information within the period and manner specified in the notice
An authorised officer may require a person to produce documents within the period and manner specified in the notice
An authorised officer may require a person to make copies of documents and produce those copies within the period and manner specified in the notice
A person must comply with a notice given under subsection (2)

sections.source_quote: The authorised officer may, by written notice given to the person, (a) require the person to give to the authorised officer, within the period and in the manner specified in the notice, any such information; or (b) require the person to produce to the authorised officer, within the period and in the manner specified in the notice, any such documents; or (c) require the person to make copies of any such documents and to produce to the authorised officer, within the period and in the manner specified in the notice, those copies.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalty provisions for non-compliance with notices
sections.summary: Subsection (3A) is designated as a civil penalty provision. A notice under subsection (2) must specify the consequences of non-compliance with the requirements outlined in subsection (3). This section establishes the legal effect of notices issued under the Act, ensuring clarity on penalties for failure to adhere to specified obligations.

requirements.text: Subsection (3A) is a civil penalty provision

sections.source_quote: Subsection (3A) is a civil penalty provision.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Examination procedures for information gathering
sections.summary: This section outlines the procedures for authorized officers to inspect and copy documents produced under the Act. It specifies that authorized officers may retain documents for a reasonable period, provide certified copies to the rightful owner, and ensure access to documents for inspection and copying. The certified copies are legally valid in all courts and tribunals.

requirements.text: An authorized officer may inspect a document produced under this Part and may make and retain copies of, or take and retain extracts from, such a document
The person otherwise entitled to possession of the document is entitled to be supplied, as soon as practicable, with a copy certified by the authorized officer to be a true copy
The certified copy must be received in all courts and tribunals as evidence as if it were the original

Until a certified copy is supplied, the authorized officer must provide the person otherwise entitled to possession of the document, or a person authorized by that person, reasonable access to the document for the purposes of inspecting and making copies of, or taking extracts from, the document

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer may inspect a document produced under this Part and may make and retain copies of, or take and retain extracts from, such a document.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's authority to obtain information and documents
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO may issue notices requiring individuals to produce documents or appear for examination if they believe on reasonable grounds that the information is relevant to compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules, or to offences under the Crimes Act 1914 or Criminal Code. Failure to comply with such notices is an offence punishable by imprisonment for up to two years or 100 penalty units.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may require a person to produce documents within the period and manner specified in the notice
The AUSTRAC CEO may require a person to appear before an examiner at the time and place specified in the notice

sections.source_quote: A person commits an offence if the person intentionally or recklessly fails to comply with a notice under subsection (2).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Examination procedures for compliance with notices
sections.summary: This section details the requirements for individuals appearing before examiners, including the administration of oaths or affirmations, the relevance of questions to compliance with the Act or offences under the Crimes Act 1914, and the penalties for refusing to comply with examination requirements. Refusal to answer relevant questions is an offence punishable by up to two years imprisonment.

requirements.text: The examiner may examine the person on oath or affirmation and may require the person to take an oath or make an affirmation
The oath or affirmation to be taken or made by the examinee is an oath or affirmation that the statements will be true
A person commits an offence of strict liability if they refuse or fail to comply with a requirement made under subsection (1)
The examiner may require the person to answer a question relevant to compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules
A person commits an offence if they intentionally or recklessly refuse or fail to comply with a requirement made under subsection (4)

sections.source_quote: The examiner may examine the person on oath or affirmation and may, for that purpose: require the person to either take an oath or make an affirmation

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Privacy during examinations
sections.summary: Examinations must take place in private, with the examiner able to specify who may be present. Unauthorized individuals present at the examination commit an offence of strict liability, punishable by 30 penalty units. This section ensures that examinations are conducted in a controlled environment to protect the integrity of the process.

requirements.text: An examination under this Division must take place in private and the examiner may give directions about who may be present during it

sections.source_quote: An examination under this Division must take place in private and the examiner may give directions about who may be present during it

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: This section allows for examinations to be conducted either physically, virtually, or a combination of both. The examiner must ensure that virtual examination technology is used reasonably and may appoint a single time and place for the examination if multiple venues are used. Virtual examination technology is defined as technology enabling participation without physical presence.

requirements.text: The examiner may decide to hold an examination at one or more physical venues, using virtual examination technology, or using virtual technology only
The examiner must ensure that the use of virtual examination technology is reasonable
If the examination is held at multiple physical venues, the examiner may appoint a single place and time for the examination

sections.source_quote: The examiner may decide to hold an examination under this Division: at one or more physical venues; or at one or more physical venues and using virtual examination technology that allows a person to appear at all or part of the examination without being physically present at the examination; or using virtual examination technology only.

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: The examiner may create a written record of statements made during an examination if the examinee requests it. sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for documenting examinations and obtaining copies of records, relevant for ensuring proper documentation of data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: The examiner may, and must if the examinee so requests, cause a record to be made of statements made at an examination

The examiner may impose conditions on providing copies of written records

sections.source_quote: 172G (1) The examiner may, and must if the examinee so requests, cause a record to be made of statements made at an examination under this Division.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Examination records: sharing with legal representatives

sections.dtia_summary: This section governs when examination records can be shared with legal representatives, relevant for ensuring proper handling of sensitive financial data during investigations.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Self-incrimination protections for examination participants
sections.summary: Individuals cannot refuse to answer questions, produce documents, or sign records on self-incrimination grounds. sections.dtia_summary: This section provides self-incrimination protections for individuals during examinations, relevant for ensuring data subjects' rights are respected during data collection processes.

requirements.text: Individuals cannot refuse to answer questions, produce documents, or sign records on self-incrimination grounds
If an individual claims self-incrimination before making a statement, the statement is not admissible in evidence against them in proceedings

sections.source_quote: 172K (1) For the purposes of this Division, it is not a reasonable excuse for an individual to refuse or fail: (a) to answer a question; or (b) to produce a document; or (c) to sign a record; in accordance with a requirement made of the individual under this Division, on the ground that answering the question, producing the document or signing the record might tend to incriminate the individual or expose the individual to a penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Admissibility of examination statements in proceedings
sections.summary: Statements made during examinations are admissible in proceedings unless they are inadmissible due to self-incrimination protections, lack of relevance, or the person objects to the statement. Signed records are prima facie evidence of the statements made, but other evidence can still be admitted.
sections.dtia_summary: This section determines when examination statements are admissible in legal proceedings, relevant for ensuring data transfers are properly documented and legally defensible.

requirements.text: Statements made during examinations are admissible in proceedings unless they are inadmissible due to self-incrimination protections, lack of relevance, or the person objects
Signed records are prima facie evidence of the statements made

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Weight of evidence from examination statements
sections.summary: The weight of examination statements as evidence is determined by how recent the statement is, whether the person had reasons to conceal or misrepresent material matters, and other circumstances affecting the statement's accuracy. The evidence is admissible if the person is not called as a witness.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses how examination statements are weighted in legal proceedings, relevant for ensuring data transfers are properly documented and legally defensible.

requirements.text: The weight of examination statements as evidence is determined by how recent the statement is

sections.source_quote: 172P (2) In deciding how much weight (if any) to give to the statement as evidence of a matter, regard is to be had to: (a) how long after the matters to which it related the statement was made; and (b) any reason the person may have had for concealing or misrepresenting a material matter; and (c) any other circumstances from which it is reasonable to draw an inference about how accurate the statement is.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Objection to admission of examination statements
sections.summary: A party may object to the admission of examination statements in proceedings by providing written notice at least 14 days before the hearing. The objection must specify the statements to be admitted and the purpose of the objection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for objections to the admission of examination statements, relevant for ensuring data transfers are properly documented and legally defensible.

sections.source_quote: 172P (1) A party (the adducing party) to a proceeding referred to in subsection (2) may, not less than 14 days before the first day of the hearing of the proceeding, give to another party to the proceeding written notice that the adducing party: (a) will apply to have admitted in evidence in the proceeding specified statements made at an examination under this Division; and (b) for that purpose, will apply to have evidence of those statements admitted in the proceeding.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Objection to evidence admission in proceedings
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for parties to object to specific statements being admitted as evidence in proceedings. Within 14 days of a notice under subsection (1), a party may submit written objections detailing grounds for refusing admission. The court may determine objections before or during the hearing. Objections must be properly documented and copied to the relevant body to ensure procedural compliance.

requirements.text: A notice under subsection (1) must set out, or be accompanied by writing that sets out, the specified statements

Within 14 days after a notice is given under subsection (1), the other party may specify, in relation to each of those statements, the grounds of objection
The period prescribed by subsection (4) may be extended by the court or tribunal or by agreement between the parties concerned

On receiving a notice given under subsection (4), the adducing party must give to the court or tribunal or other body a copy of the notice under subsection (1) and any writing that subsection (3) required to accompany that notice
On receiving a notice given under subsection (4), the adducing party must give to the court or tribunal or other body a copy of the notice under subsection (4)
If subsection (6) is complied with, the court or tribunal or other body may determine the objections as a preliminary point before the hearing of the proceeding begins
If subsection (6) is complied with, the court or tribunal or other body may defer determination of the objections until the hearing

If a notice has been given in accordance with subsections (1) and (3), the other party is not entitled to object at the hearing of the proceeding to a statement specified in the notice being admitted in evidence unless the other party has objected to the statement being so admitted

If a notice has been given in accordance with subsections (1) and (3), the other party is not entitled to object at the hearing of the proceeding to a statement specified in the notice being admitted in evidence unless the court or tribunal or other body gives the other party leave to object to the statement being so admitted

sections.source_quote: 305 (3) A notice under subsection (1) must set out, or be accompanied by writing that sets out, the specified statements.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Admissibility of evidence in proceedings

requirements.text: Nothing in this Division renders evidence inadmissible in a proceeding in circumstances where it would have been admissible in that proceeding if this Division had not been enacted

sections.source_quote: 306 (1) Nothing in this Division renders evidence inadmissible in a proceeding in circumstances where it would have been admissible in that proceeding if this Division had not been enacted.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enforcement mechanisms for compliance
sections.summary: This section details enforcement mechanisms including pecuniary penalties for contraventions, powers for authorized officers to issue infringement notices for unreported cross-border monetary movements, monitoring by the AUSTRAC CEO, remedial directions, injunctions, enforceable undertakings, and powers for customs and police officers to question, search and arrest in connection with cross-border monetary instruments.

requirements.text: Pecuniary penalties are payable for contraventions of civil penalty provisions
Authorised officers, customs officers and police officers may issue infringement notices for unreported cross-border movements of monetary instruments
The AUSTRAC CEO is to monitor compliance by reporting entities with their obligations under this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules
The AUSTRAC CEO may give a remedial direction to a reporting entity that has contravened a civil penalty provision
The Federal Court may grant injunctions in relation to contraventions of civil penalty provisions
The AUSTRAC CEO may accept enforceable undertakings

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalty provisions and contraventions
sections.summary: This section outlines the definition of ancillary contraventions of civil penalty provisions, including attempts to contravene, aiding or abetting contraventions, inducing contraventions, being knowingly concerned in contraventions, and conspiring to effect contraventions. It also details civil penalty orders, the determination of pecuniary penalties, maximum penalties for bodies corporate and individuals, and the handling of multiple contraventions.

requirements.text: A person must not attempt to contravene a civil penalty provision (other than this subsection)
A person must not aid, abet, counsel or procure a contravention of a civil penalty provision (other than this subsection)
A person must not induce, whether by threats or promises or otherwise, a contravention of a civil penalty provision (other than this subsection)
A person must not be in any way, directly or indirectly, knowingly concerned in, or party to, a contravention of a civil penalty provision (other than this subsection)
A person must not conspire with others to effect a contravention of a civil penalty provision (other than this subsection)
If the Federal Court is satisfied that a person has contravened a civil penalty provision, the Federal Court may order the person to pay the Commonwealth a pecuniary penalty
An order under subsection (1) is to be known as a civil penalty order
In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court must have regard to all relevant matters, including the nature and extent of the contravention
In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court must have regard to all relevant matters, including the nature and extent of any loss or damage suffered as a result of the contravention
In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court must have regard to all relevant matters, including the circumstances in which the contravention took place

In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court must have regard to all relevant matters, including whether the person has previously been found by the Federal Court in proceedings under this Act to have engaged in any similar conduct

In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court may have regard to whether the person has previously been found by a court in proceedings under a law of a State or Territory to have engaged in any similar conduct
In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court may have regard to whether the person has previously been found by a court in a foreign country to have engaged in any similar conduct

In determining the pecuniary penalty, the Federal Court may have regard to whether the person has previously been found by a court in proceedings under the repealed Financial Transaction Reports Act 1988 to have engaged in any similar conduct
The pecuniary penalty payable by a body corporate must not exceed 100,000 penalty units
The pecuniary penalty payable by a person other than a body corporate must not exceed 20,000 penalty units

If conduct constitutes a contravention of 2 or more civil penalty provisions, proceedings may be instituted under this section against a person in relation to the contravention of any one or more of those provisions
The person is not liable to more than one pecuniary penalty under this section in respect of the same conduct

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalty enforcement procedures
sections.summary: This section details procedures for civil penalty orders, including who may apply for orders, the possibility of hearing multiple proceedings together, time limits for applications (6 years after contravention), application of civil evidence and procedure rules, and the prohibition of civil penalty orders if criminal proceedings have been initiated for substantially similar conduct.

requirements.text: Only the AUSTRAC CEO may apply for a civil penalty order
Subsection (1) does not exclude the operation of the Director of Public Prosecutions Act 1983
The Federal Court may direct that 2 or more proceedings for civil penalty orders are to be heard together
Proceedings for a civil penalty order may be started no later than 6 years after the contravention
The Federal Court must apply the rules of evidence and procedure for civil matters when hearing proceedings for a civil penalty order

The Federal Court must not make a civil penalty order against a person for a contravention if the person has been convicted of an offence constituted by conduct that is substantially the same as the conduct constituting the contravention

sections.source_quote: 310 (1) Only the AUSTRAC CEO may apply for a civil penalty order.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Conduct equivalence in civil and criminal proceedings
sections.summary: This section establishes that criminal proceedings can be initiated for conduct substantially identical to a civil penalty contravention, regardless of prior civil penalty orders. It specifies that proceedings for an order may be resumed if the person is not convicted of the offence, otherwise they are dismissed. The evidence given in civil proceedings is not admissible in criminal proceedings if the conduct is substantially the same and the evidence was previously provided in civil proceedings.

requirements.text: Criminal proceedings may be started against a person for conduct substantially the same as conduct constituting a civil penalty contravention regardless of civil penalty orders.
Proceedings for an order may be resumed if the person is not convicted of the offence; otherwise, proceedings are dismissed.
Evidence given in proceedings for a civil penalty order is not admissible in criminal proceedings if the conduct is substantially the same.

sections.source_quote: Criminal proceedings may be started against a person for conduct that is substantially the same as conduct constituting a contravention of a civil penalty provision regardless of whether a civil penalty order has been made against the person.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notices for certain contraventions
sections.summary: This section outlines when and how infringement notices can be issued for specific contraventions. It specifies that an authorised officer may issue an infringement notice if they have reasonable grounds to believe a person has contravened an infringement notice provision. The notice must be issued within 12 months of the alleged contravention and must include specific details such as the person's name, the officer's name, and brief contravention details. The notice must also state that payment of the penalty within 28 days prevents criminal or civil proceedings.

requirements.text: An authorised officer may issue an infringement notice if they have reasonable grounds to believe a person has contravened an infringement notice provision.
Infringement notices must be issued within 12 months after the alleged contravention.
Customs or police officers must forward a copy of the infringement notice to AUSTRAC CEO within 5 business days.

Infringement notices must include the name of the person to whom the notice is given, the name of the person who gave the notice, brief details of the alleged contravention, and a statement that payment of the penalty within 28 days prevents criminal or civil proceedings.

sections.source_quote: If an authorised officer, a customs officer or a police officer has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has contravened an infringement notice provision, the officer may give the person an infringement notice relating to the contravention.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notice content and conditions
sections.summary: This section details the specific content requirements for infringement notices, including the need to specify the person's name, the officer's name, brief contravention details, and a statement that payment of the penalty within 28 days prevents criminal or civil proceedings. It also specifies that the notice must be issued within 12 months of the alleged contravention and that customs or police officers must forward a copy to AUSTRAC CEO within 5 business days.

requirements.text: Infringement notices must set out the name of the person to whom the notice is given.
Infringement notices must set out the name of the person who gave the notice.
Infringement notices must set out brief details relating to the alleged contravention, including the date of the alleged contravention.

sections.source_quote: An infringement notice must: set out the name of the person to whom the notice is given; set out the name of the person who gave the notice; set out brief details relating to the alleged contravention of the infringement notice provision, including the date of the alleged contravention;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notice penalty amounts
sections.summary: This section specifies the penalty amounts for infringement notices based on the type of contravention and whether the entity is a body corporate or a person. For body corporates, the penalty is 60 penalty units unless specified in the AML/CTF Rules, where it can be up to 120 units. For individuals, the penalty is 12 penalty units unless specified in the AML/CTF Rules, where it can be up to 24 units. The AML/CTF Rules may specify penalty units for specific contraventions.

requirements.text: Penalty for body corporates is 60 penalty units unless specified in AML/CTF Rules (up to 120 units).
Penalty for individuals is 12 penalty units unless specified in AML/CTF Rules (up to 24 units).
AML/CTF Rules may specify penalty units for specific contraventions.

sections.source_quote: The penalty to be specified in an infringement notice for an alleged contravention of subsection 51B(1), 51F(1), 53(2), 74(1), (1A), (1B) or (1C), 75M(1), 76A(1) or (2) or 76P(1) by a body corporate must be a pecuniary penalty equal to: 60 penalty units.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Penalty amounts for infringement notices
sections.summary: This section specifies pecuniary penalties for infringement notices. A body corporate faces a penalty of 60 penalty units for contraventions of designated infringement notice provisions, while individuals face 12 penalty units. The penalties are fixed amounts without additional conditions.

requirements.text: The penalty for a body corporate contravention must be 60 penalty units
The penalty for an individual contravention must be 12 penalty units

sections.source_quote: The penalty to be specified in an infringement notice for an alleged contravention of a designated infringement notice provision by a body corporate must be a pecuniary penalty equal to 60 penalty units.

sections.source_quote: If: (a) the penalty specified in the infringement notice is paid; and (b) the infringement notice is withdrawn after the penalty is paid; the Commonwealth is liable to refund the penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC monitoring of compliance
sections.summary: This section requires the AUSTRAC CEO to monitor and report compliance by reporting entities with their obligations under the Act, regulations, and AML/CTF Rules. The CEO may share breach reports with the Minister if the breach is relevant to government functions. Actions taken under this section do not constitute legal liability.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO must monitor compliance by reporting entities with their obligations under the Act, regulations, and AML/CTF Rules

Actions taken under this section do not constitute legal liability

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Remedial directions for compliance
sections.summary: This section allows the AUSTRAC CEO to issue written directions to reporting entities that have contravened civil penalty provisions. Directions may require corrective actions to prevent future contraventions or remedy specific contraventions by submitting reports within specified timeframes. The CEO must assess risks and ensure proportionality before issuing directions.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may issue written directions requiring reporting entities to take specified actions to prevent future contraventions
The AUSTRAC CEO may issue written directions requiring reporting entities to remedy contraventions by submitting reports within specified timeframes
The AUSTRAC CEO must assess risks arising from contraventions before issuing directions
The AUSTRAC CEO must determine that giving a direction is proportionate to the circumstances

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO may give the reporting entity a written direction requiring the reporting entity to do one or both of the following: (a) to take specified action directed towards ensuring that the reporting entity does not contravene the civil penalty provision, or is unlikely to contravene the civil penalty provision, in the future; (b) in the case of a contravention of subsection 43(2), 46(2) or (5), 46A(2) or 47(2)—to take specified action to remedy the contravention by giving the relevant report to the AUSTRAC CEO within a period specified in the direction.

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which remedial directions can be issued. requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO must not issue directions for contraventions that occurred more than 24 months before the direction

The AUSTRAC CEO must determine that giving a direction is appropriate and proportionate to the circumstances

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Injunctions for contraventions
sections.summary: This section empowers the Federal Court to grant restraining or performance injunctions against persons engaging in conduct that contravenes civil penalty provisions. Injunctions may require persons to cease contraventions or perform specific acts to comply with the provisions. Interim injunctions can be granted before a full hearing.

requirements.text: The Federal Court may grant restraining injunctions against persons engaging in contraventions of civil penalty provisions
The Federal Court may grant performance injunctions requiring persons to perform acts that contravene civil penalty provisions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Injunctions: Discharge and Variation Powers
sections.summary: The Federal Court may discharge or vary injunctions granted under this Division. The Court can grant restraining injunctions if the person has engaged in prohibited conduct or if there is a likelihood of future conduct causing substantial damage. Performance injunctions require the person to take specified actions if they have refused or failed to do so, or if there is a likelihood of future refusal.

requirements.text: The Federal Court may discharge or vary an injunction granted under this Division.
The Court may grant restraining injunctions if the person has engaged in conduct of that kind.
The Court may grant restraining injunctions if it is likely the person will engage in conduct of that kind.
The Court may grant performance injunctions if the person has refused or failed to do the act or thing.
The Court may grant performance injunctions if it is likely the person will refuse or fail to do the act or thing.

sections.source_quote: The Federal Court may discharge or vary an injunction granted under this Division. The power of the Federal Court under this Division to grant an injunction restraining a person from engaging in conduct of a particular kind may be exercised: (a) if the Court is satisfied that the person has engaged in conduct of that kind—whether or not it appears to the Court that the person intends to engage again, or to continue to engage, in conduct of that kind; or (b) if it appears to the Court that, if an injunction is not granted, it is likely that the person will engage in conduct of that kind—whether or not the person has previously engaged in conduct of that kind and whether or not there is an imminent danger of substantial damage to any person if the person engages in conduct of that kind.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Injunctions: Powers Unaffected by Other Laws
sections.summary: The Federal Court's powers under this Division are in addition to and not instead of any other powers it may have under this Act or other laws. This ensures that injunction-related powers are not restricted by other provisions of the legislation.

requirements.text: The powers conferred on the Federal Court under this Division are in addition to, and not instead of, any other powers of the Court, whether conferred by this Act or otherwise.

sections.source_quote: The powers conferred on the Federal Court under this Division are in addition to, and not instead of, any other powers of the Court, whether conferred by this Act or otherwise.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enforceable Undertakings: Acceptance and Management
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO may accept written undertakings for compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules. These undertakings can require specified actions, refraining from actions, or ensuring compliance with the law. The AUSTRAC CEO must delete from the copy information that should not be released due to public interest.
The AUSTRAC CEO must delete from the copy information that consists of personal details of an individual.

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO may accept any of the following undertakings: (a) a written undertaking given by a person that the person will, in order to comply with this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules, take specified action; (b) a written undertaking given by a person that the person will, in order to comply with this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules, refrain from taking specified action; (c) a written undertaking given by a person that the person will take specified action directed towards ensuring that the person does not contravene this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules, or is unlikely to contravene this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules, in the future.

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Cross-border Monetary Instruments: Declaration and Seizure
sections.summary: Persons leaving or arriving in Australia must declare monetary instruments they possess, the total value, and whether a report under section 53 has been made. Police or customs officers may seize monetary instruments and make copies of bearer negotiable instruments or items specified by the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts cross-border transfers of monetary instruments by requiring declarations and potential seizures, which may affect data transfer assessments involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: A person about to leave Australia must declare whether they have monetary instruments with them.

A person about to leave Australia must declare whether a report under section 53 has been given for monetary instruments.
A person about to leave Australia must produce monetary instruments to the officer.
A person arriving in Australia must declare whether they have monetary instruments with them.
A person arriving in Australia must declare the sum of monetary instrument amounts.
A person arriving in Australia must declare whether a report under section 53 has been given for monetary instruments.
A person arriving in Australia must produce monetary instruments to the officer.
A police officer or customs officer may seize a monetary instrument produced to them.
A police officer or customs officer may make a copy of a bearer negotiable instrument or item specified by the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.source_quote: A person who is: (a) about to leave Australia; or (b) in an embarkation area for the purpose of leaving Australia; must, if required to do so by a police officer or a customs officer: (c) declare whether or not the person has with him or her any monetary instruments; and (d) declare the sum of the monetary instrument amounts for the monetary instruments that the person has with him or her; and (e) declare whether or not, to the best of the person's knowledge and belief, a report under section 53 has been given in respect of any monetary instruments that the person has with him or her; and (f) produce to the officer any monetary instruments that the person has with him or her.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Search conditions for monetary instruments during border crossings
sections.summary: This section outlines when police or customs officers may search individuals for monetary instruments. sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies conditions under which monetary instruments can be searched during border crossings, relevant to cross-border data transfers involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: The person is about to leave Australia or has arrived in Australia
The person is about to board or leave, or has boarded or left, any ship or aircraft
The officer has reasonable grounds to suspect the person has a monetary instrument requiring reporting under section 53

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.dtia_summary: This section defines monetary instruments of interest, relevant to cross-border data transfers involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: A monetary instrument may be of interest if it may be relevant to the investigation of, or prosecution of a person for, an offence against a law of the Commonwealth or of a State or Territory
A monetary instrument may be of interest if it may be of assistance in the enforcement of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 or regulations under that Act

A monetary instrument may be of interest if it may be of assistance in the enforcement of a law of a State or Territory that corresponds to the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 or regulations under that Act

sections.source_quote: 332 (a) may be relevant to the investigation of, or prosecution of a person for, an offence against a law of the Commonwealth or of a State or Territory; or (b) may be of assistance in the enforcement of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 or regulations under that Act; or (c) may be of assistance in the enforcement of a law of a State or Territory that corresponds to the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 or regulations under that Act.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notices to reporting entities for financial investigations
sections.summary: This section authorises specific individuals to issue written notices to reporting entities, requiring them to provide information about their Australian permanent establishments and designated services. Notices must be issued only if the authorised person reasonably believes action under the Act is required or proceedings are underway.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the process for issuing notices to reporting entities, relevant to cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.
sections.authority: AUSTRAC CEO, authorised officers, Australian Federal Police, Australian Crime Commission

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO, an authorised officer, the Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police, a Deputy Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police, a senior executive AFP employee, the Chief Executive Officer of the Australian Crime Commission, an examiner of the Australian Crime Commission, or an approved examiner may give notices

An authorised person may give a written notice requiring information or documents relevant to determining whether the person provides designated services at or through a permanent establishment in Australia
An authorised person may give a written notice requiring information or documents to ascertain details relating to a permanent establishment in Australia
An authorised person may give a written notice requiring information or documents to ascertain details relating to designated services provided at or through a permanent establishment in Australia
A person must not give a notice unless they reasonably believe giving the notice is required to determine whether to take action under this Act or in relation to proceedings under this Act

sections.source_quote: 333 (1) Each of the following persons is authorised to give notices under this section: (a) the AUSTRAC CEO; (b) an authorised officer; (c) the Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police; (d) a Deputy Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police; (e) a senior executive AFP employee (within the meaning of the Australian Federal Police Act 1979) who is: (i) a member of the Australian Federal Police; and (ii) authorised in writing by the Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police for the purposes of this section; (f) the Chief Executive Officer of the Australian Crime Commission; (g) an examiner of the Australian Crime Commission; (h) an approved examiner (within the meaning of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemption from self-incrimination in reporting under financial intelligence laws
sections.summary: A person is not excused from providing information or producing documents under financial intelligence reporting requirements due to potential self-incrimination or penalties. This applies to information given or documents produced, but exceptions exist for proceedings under the Act, offences under the Act, or Criminal Code proceedings related to the Act. The provision ensures reporting entities must comply with notice requirements without fear of legal repercussions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that reporting entities cannot claim self-incrimination as a defense when providing financial intelligence information, ensuring compliance with data transfer obligations under the Act without legal barriers.

requirements.text: A person is not excused from giving information or producing a document under section 202 on the ground that the information or the production of the document might tend to incriminate the person or expose the person to a penalty

The information given or the document produced; or giving the information or producing the document; may be subject to proceedings under this Act, offences under this Act, or Criminal Code proceedings related to this Act

sections.source_quote: A person is not excused from giving information or producing a document under section 202 on the ground that the information or the production of the document might tend to incriminate the person or expose the person to a penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Prohibition on disclosing financial intelligence notices to unauthorized parties
sections.summary: Reporting entities must not disclose the existence or nature of a financial intelligence notice unless specific exceptions apply, such as disclosures to legal practitioners for legal advice, IGIS officials performing duties, or members of a reporting group. This ensures confidentiality of notices while allowing necessary internal and external communications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the disclosure of financial intelligence notices to unauthorized parties, which impacts data sharing and transfer obligations when handling sensitive financial information across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A reporting entity must not disclose the existence or nature of a notice if the notice specifies that information about the notice must not be disclosed
Disclosures to legal practitioners for legal advice are exempt
Disclosures to IGIS officials for their functions are exempt
Disclosures to other reporting group members are exempt

sections.source_quote: A person is not excused from giving information or producing a document under section 202 on the ground that the information or the production of the document might tend to incriminate the person or expose the person to a penalty.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC's administrative functions and authority
sections.summary: AUSTRAC is established with a Chief Executive Officer who has authority to compile and analyze financial intelligence data, use computer programs for administrative actions, and make AML/CTF Rules. This section outlines AUSTRAC's administrative framework and its role in financial intelligence operations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines AUSTRAC's administrative powers, including data compilation and analysis, which are critical for financial intelligence operations and data transfer compliance within Australia's financial sector.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC is continued in existence
AUSTRAC has a Chief Executive Officer
The AUSTRAC CEO's functions include compiling and analyzing AUSTRAC information
The AUSTRAC CEO may arrange for the use of computer programs for administrative actions
The AUSTRAC CEO may make AML/CTF Rules

sections.source_quote: AUSTRAC is continued in existence. There is to be a Chief Executive Officer of AUSTRAC. The AUSTRAC CEO's functions include the compilation and analysis of AUSTRAC information.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Establishment of AUSTRAC as a Commonwealth entity
sections.summary: AUSTRAC continues as a Commonwealth entity with a Chief Executive Officer and staff, operating without legal identity separate from the Commonwealth. It is designated as a listed entity for financial law purposes with the CEO as the accountable authority, and its functions include financial intelligence operations and compliance assistance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes AUSTRAC's legal structure and role in financial intelligence, which impacts data handling and transfer obligations within Australia's financial sector.

requirements.text: AUSTRAC continues in existence by force of this Act
AUSTRAC may be known as AUSTRAC
AUSTRAC consists of the CEO and staff
AUSTRAC is a listed entity for financial law purposes
The AUSTRAC CEO is the accountable authority of AUSTRAC
AUSTRAC's purposes include financial intelligence functions

sections.source_quote: The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre established under the repealed Financial Transaction Reports Act 1988 continues in existence by force of this subsection, under and subject to the provisions of this Act.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's functions and responsibilities
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO retains, compiles, analyzes, and disseminates financial intelligence data to combat money laundering and terrorism financing. The CEO also provides advice to authorized entities, assists reporting entities with compliance, and facilitates timely access to financial information for intelligence functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the AUSTRAC CEO's critical role in financial intelligence operations, directly impacting data transfer and processing obligations for financial institutions handling cross-border data.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO must retain, compile, analyze and disseminate AUSTRAC information
The AUSTRAC CEO must provide access to AUSTRAC information to support domestic and international efforts to combat money laundering and terrorism financing
The AUSTRAC CEO must provide advice and assistance to authorized entities regarding AUSTRAC information
The AUSTRAC CEO must advise and assist reporting entities with compliance obligations
The AUSTRAC CEO must facilitate timely access to financial information for intelligence functions

sections.source_quote: The functions of the AUSTRAC CEO are: to retain, compile, analyse and disseminate AUSTRAC information; to provide access to, and to share, AUSTRAC information to support domestic and international efforts to combat money laundering and terrorism financing and other serious crimes; to provide advice and assistance, in relation to AUSTRAC information, to the persons and agencies who are authorised to access AUSTRAC information under Part 11; to advise and assist reporting entities in relation to their obligations under this Act, the regulations and the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's compliance with financial system integrity and risk considerations
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO must consider financial system integrity, crime reduction, minimal regulatory burdens, risk-based approaches, competitive neutrality, competition, economic efficiency, privacy, and other relevant matters when performing functions under this Act. Reporting entities must comply with these considerations to ensure effective regulation without undue financial or administrative strain.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes risk-based regulatory considerations that impact how financial data is handled across borders, particularly in money laundering and terrorism financing contexts.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to the integrity of the financial system
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to crime reduction
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to ensuring regulatory considerations do not impose unnecessary financial and administrative burdens on reporting entities
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to adopting a risk-based approach
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to competitive neutrality
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to competition
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to economic efficiency
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to privacy
The AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to other relevant matters as considered by the AUSTRAC CEO

sections.source_quote: In performing the AUSTRAC CEO's functions under this Act, the AUSTRAC CEO must have regard to the following: (a) the integrity of the financial system; (b) crime reduction; (c) the desirability of ensuring that regulatory considerations are addressed in a way that does not impose unnecessary financial and administrative burdens on reporting entities; (d) the desirability of adopting a risk-based approach; (e) competitive neutrality; (f) competition; (g) economic efficiency; (h) privacy; (i) such other matters (if any) as the AUSTRAC CEO considers relevant.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's function validity and policy principles
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO's functions remain valid even if they fail to comply with certain requirements. The Minister may provide written policy principles to the AUSTRAC CEO, which must be tabled in Parliament within 15 sitting days and followed by the AUSTRAC CEO.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures policy principles do not affect the validity of AUSTRAC CEO's functions and requires transparency in parliamentary processes for regulatory guidance.

requirements.text: The Minister may give written policy principles to the AUSTRAC CEO about the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions
The Minister must cause a copy of the policy principles to be tabled in each House of the Parliament within 15 sitting days of that House after the day on which they were given to the AUSTRAC CEO
The AUSTRAC CEO must comply with the policy principles (if any) when performing the AUSTRAC CEO's functions

sections.source_quote: The Minister may give written policy principles to the AUSTRAC CEO about the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions. The Minister must cause a copy of the policy principles to be tabled in each House of the Parliament within 15 sitting days of that House after the day on which they were given to the AUSTRAC CEO. The AUSTRAC CEO must comply with the policy principles (if any) when performing the AUSTRAC CEO's functions.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's remuneration and allowances
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO's remuneration is determined by the Remuneration Tribunal or the Minister, with allowances prescribed by the Minister. This section is subject to the Remuneration Tribunal Act 1973.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the financial terms of the AUSTRAC CEO's appointment, which may impact how financial data is managed across borders through regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the remuneration that is determined by the Remuneration Tribunal
The AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the remuneration that is determined by the Minister
The AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the allowances that are prescribed
This section has effect subject to the Remuneration Tribunal Act 1973

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the remuneration that is determined by the Remuneration Tribunal. If no determination of that remuneration by the Tribunal is in operation, the AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the remuneration that is determined by the Minister. The AUSTRAC CEO is to be paid the allowances that are prescribed. This section has effect subject to the Remuneration Tribunal Act 1973.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's termination and conflict of interest provisions
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO may be terminated for misbehaviour, physical or mental incapacity, bankruptcy, insolvency, creditor compounding, assignments for creditors, absence from duty, paid employment outside duties, failure to disclose interests, or unsatisfactory performance. The Minister must determine if conflicts of interest pose significant risks and require disposal of those interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines termination conditions and conflict of interest management for the AUSTRAC CEO, which impacts how financial data is handled across borders through regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO for misbehaviour or physical or mental incapacity
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO becomes bankrupt
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO applies to take the benefit of any law for the relief of bankrupt or insolvent debtors
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO compounds with his or her creditors
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO makes an assignment of his or her remuneration for the benefit of his or her creditors
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO is absent from duty for 14 consecutive days or 28 days in any 12 months
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO engages in paid employment outside the duties of his or her office without the Minister's approval
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the AUSTRAC CEO fails to comply with section 29 of the Public Governance, Performance and Accountability Act 2013
The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if the Minister is satisfied that the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO has been unsatisfactory for a significant period

If the Minister becomes aware that the AUSTRAC CEO has an interest that could conflict with the proper performance of duties, the Minister must make a written determination whether the interest poses a significant risk of conflict
If the Minister determines that an interest poses a significant risk of conflict, the Minister must require the AUSTRAC CEO to dispose of that interest within a period specified by the Minister
If the Minister requires the AUSTRAC CEO to dispose of an interest and the AUSTRAC CEO refuses or fails to comply, the Minister must terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO

sections.source_quote: The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO for misbehaviour or physical or mental incapacity. The Minister may terminate the appointment of the AUSTRAC CEO if: (a) the AUSTRAC CEO: (i) becomes bankrupt; (ii) applies to take the benefit of any law for the relief of bankrupt or insolvent debtors; (iii) compounds with his or her creditors; (iv) makes an assignment of his or her remuneration for the benefit of his or her creditors; (b) the AUSTRAC CEO is absent from duty, except on leave of absence, for 14 consecutive days or for 28 days in any 12 months; (c) the AUSTRAC CEO engages, except with the Minister's approval, in paid employment outside the duties of his or her office; (d) the AUSTRAC CEO fails, without reasonable excuse, to comply with section 29 of the Public Governance, Performance and Accountability Act 2013 (which deals with the duty to disclose interests) or rules made for the purposes of that section; (e) the Minister is satisfied that the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO has been unsatisfactory for a significant period.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's other terms and conditions
sections.summary: The AUSTRAC CEO holds office on terms and conditions determined by the Minister for matters not covered by this Act. The Minister may appoint acting appointments during vacancies or absences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows flexibility in the AUSTRAC CEO's appointment terms, which may impact how financial data is managed across borders through regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO holds office on the terms and conditions (if any) in relation to matters not covered by this Act that are determined by the Minister

sections.source_quote: The AUSTRAC CEO holds office on the terms and conditions (if any) in relation to matters not covered by this Act that are determined by the Minister.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Secretary's requirement for AUSTRAC CEO to provide information
sections.summary: The Secretary may require the AUSTRAC CEO to prepare a document setting out specified information related to the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions and provide a copy to the Secretary within a specified period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Secretary to request information from the AUSTRAC CEO, which may impact how financial data is handled across borders through regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Secretary may, by written notice given to the AUSTRAC CEO, require the AUSTRAC CEO to prepare a document setting out specified information relating to the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions
The AUSTRAC CEO must give a copy of the document to the Secretary within the period specified in the notice

sections.source_quote: The Secretary may, by written notice given to the AUSTRAC CEO, require the AUSTRAC CEO to: (a) prepare a document setting out specified information relating to the performance of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions; and (b) give a copy of the document to the Secretary within the period specified in the notice.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of AUSTRAC staff and Statutory Agency
sections.summary: This section defines AUSTRAC staff as persons engaged under the Public Service Act 1999. It establishes that the AUSTRAC CEO and AUSTRAC staff together form a Statutory Agency, with the CEO serving as its Head. The section clarifies the legal framework for AUSTRAC's personnel structure under Commonwealth law.

requirements.text: The staff of AUSTRAC are persons engaged under the Public Service Act 1999
The AUSTRAC CEO and the staff of AUSTRAC together constitute a Statutory Agency
The AUSTRAC CEO is the Head of that Statutory Agency

sections.source_quote:
348 Compilation
Division 4—Staff of AUSTRAC etc.
224 Staff of AUSTRAC
(1) The staff of AUSTRAC are persons engaged under the Public Service Act 1999.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of consultants and seconded personnel for AUSTRAC
sections.summary: This section defines who qualifies as consultants or persons seconded to AUSTRAC, including officers from other Commonwealth agencies, state/territory authorities, police forces, and qualified individuals from other organizations. It specifies that these personnel must be engaged for the purpose of performing AUSTRAC's functions.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may engage consultants to perform services for AUSTRAC in connection with the AUSTRAC CEO's functions
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by officers and employees of Agencies (within the meaning of the Public Service Act 1999)
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by officers and employees of authorities of the Commonwealth
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by members of the Australian Federal Police
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by officers and employees of a State or Territory
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by officers and employees of authorities of a State or Territory
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by members of the police force or police service of a State or Territory
The AUSTRAC CEO may be assisted by persons with suitable qualifications and experience who are officers or employees of some other body or organisation

sections.source_quote:

Division 4—Staff of AUSTRAC etc.

(1) The AUSTRAC CEO may, on behalf of the Commonwealth, engage consultants to perform services for AUSTRAC in connection with the performance of any of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ministerial directions to AUSTRAC CEO
sections.summary: This section grants the Minister the authority to issue written directions to the AUSTRAC CEO regarding policies or priorities for performing AUSTRAC's functions. It prohibits directing specific cases, mandates compliance with directions, and requires the Minister to table copies of directions in Parliament within 15 sitting days.

requirements.text: The Minister may give the AUSTRAC CEO a written direction about policies the AUSTRAC CEO should pursue, or priorities the AUSTRAC CEO should follow, in performing any of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions
The Minister must not give a direction under subsection (1) about a particular case
The AUSTRAC CEO must comply with a direction under subsection (1)
A direction under subsection (1) is not a legislative instrument
The Minister must cause a copy of each direction under subsection (1) to be tabled in each House of the Parliament within 15 sitting days of that House after giving the direction

sections.source_quote:

228 Directions by Minister
(1) The Minister may give the AUSTRAC CEO a written direction about policies the AUSTRAC CEO should pursue, or priorities the AUSTRAC CEO should follow, in performing any of the AUSTRAC CEO's functions.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AUSTRAC CEO's use of computers for administrative action
sections.summary: This section permits the AUSTRAC CEO to arrange for computer programs to be used under their control for administrative actions required by the AML/CTF Rules. It specifies that administrative actions taken via computer programs must align with the Act's objectives and are treated as actions taken by the AUSTRAC CEO for legal purposes.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may, in writing, arrange for the use, under the AUSTRAC CEO's control, of computer programs for any purposes for which the AUSTRAC CEO may or must take administrative action under a provision prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules

The AML/CTF Rules may prescribe a provision for the purposes of subsection (1) if the provision is any of the following: a provision of Part 3A of this Act (Reporting Entities Roll), a provision of Part 6 of this Act (the Remittance Sector Register), a provision of Part 6A of this Act (the Virtual Asset Service Provider Register), a provision of the AML/CTF Rules made for the purposes of a provision of this Act mentioned in paragraph (a), (b) or (c) of this subsection, or a provision of an instrument made under a provision of this Act mentioned in paragraph (a), (b) or (c) of this subsection
The AUSTRAC CEO must take reasonable steps to ensure that administrative action taken by the operation of a computer program under such an arrangement is consistent with the objects of this Act

Administrative action taken by the operation of a computer program under such an arrangement is, for the purposes of this Act, the AML/CTF Rules and any other instrument made under this Act, taken to be administrative action taken by the AUSTRAC CEO

sections.source_quote:
351 Compilation
Division 6A—AUSTRAC CEO may use computers to take administrative action
228A AUSTRAC CEO may use computers to take administrative action
(1) The AUSTRAC CEO may, in writing, arrange for the use, under the AUSTRAC CEO's control, of computer programs for any purposes for which the AUSTRAC CEO may or must take administrative action under a provision prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules for the purposes of this subsection.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Substitution of decisions by AUSTRAC CEO
sections.summary: This section allows the AUSTRAC CEO to substitute a decision made by computer programs for a decision they initially took, provided they are satisfied the computer-generated decision is incorrect or less preferable. It clarifies that substitutions do not require consideration and that substituted decisions take precedence over original decisions for legal purposes.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may substitute a decision for a decision the AUSTRAC CEO is taken to have made under subsection (4) if the AUSTRAC CEO is satisfied that the decision made by the operation of the computer program is not the correct or preferable decision
The AUSTRAC CEO does not have a duty to consider whether to exercise the power under subsection (5) in respect of any decision

If the AUSTRAC CEO is taken under subsection (4) to have made a decision under a provision of this Act, the AML/CTF Rules or an instrument made under this Act, and the AUSTRAC CEO substitutes a decision under subsection (5) for that decision, for the purposes of this Act, the AUSTRAC CEO is taken to have made the substitute decision under the original provision

sections.source_quote:
352 Compilation
Substituted decisions
(5) The AUSTRAC CEO may substitute a decision for a decision the AUSTRAC CEO is taken to have made under subsection (4) if the AUSTRAC CEO is satisfied that the decision made by the operation of the computer program is not the correct or preferable decision.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: AML/CTF Rules and their application
sections.summary: This section authorizes the AUSTRAC CEO to make rules (the AML/CTF Rules) that prescribe matters required or permitted by other provisions of the Act. It states that AML/CTF Rules are legislative instruments and that the Minister may direct the AUSTRAC CEO on how to exercise their powers to make these rules.

requirements.text: The AUSTRAC CEO may, by writing, make rules (the AML/CTF Rules) prescribing matters required or permitted by any other provision of this Act to be prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules
AML/CTF Rules are legislative instruments
The Minister may give the AUSTRAC CEO a written direction about the exercise of the powers conferred on the AUSTRAC CEO by subsection (1)
The AUSTRAC CEO must comply with a direction under subsection (3)

(1) The AUSTRAC CEO may, by writing, make rules (the AML/CTF Rules) prescribing matters required or permitted by any other provision of this Act to be prescribed by the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Vicarious liability for corporate conduct in legal proceedings
sections.summary: This section establishes vicarious liability for corporate conduct in legal proceedings. It states that conduct engaged in by an employee or agent within their actual or apparent authority is deemed to have been done by the corporation unless the corporation proves it took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the conduct. This applies to both criminal and civil proceedings under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing conditions under which corporate liability for employee actions can be avoided through due diligence and reasonable precautions, relevant to third-party processing and cross-border data handling scenarios.

requirements.text: Conduct engaged in by an employee or agent within actual or apparent authority is deemed to have been done by the corporation unless the corporation proves it took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence

sections.source_quote: the conduct is taken, for the purposes of a civil proceeding under, or arising out of, this Act, to have been engaged in by the corporation unless the corporation proves that it took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the conduct.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Review of AUSTRAC decisions by Administrative Review Tribunal
sections.summary: This section outlines the review process for certain AUSTRAC decisions. It specifies that decisions of the AUSTRAC CEO or delegates may be reviewed by the Administrative Review Tribunal following internal reconsideration. It lists specific reviewable decisions including registration refusals, condition impositions, and cancellations related to virtual asset service providers and remittance networks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section matters for DTIAs as it establishes a review mechanism for AUSTRAC decisions that could impact data transfer approvals, particularly for financial entities operating across borders.

requirements.text: Certain decisions of delegates of the AUSTRAC CEO may be reviewed by the Administrative Review Tribunal following internal reconsideration
Certain decisions of the AUSTRAC CEO may be reviewed by the Administrative Review Tribunal
A decision under subsection 75B(6) or section 75C to refuse registration as a remittance affiliate is reviewable
A decision under section 75E to impose conditions on registration is reviewable
A decision under section 75G to cancel registration is reviewable
A decision under subsection 76D(4) or section 76E to refuse registration as a virtual asset service provider is reviewable
A decision under section 76G to impose conditions on registration is reviewable
A decision under section 76J to cancel registration is reviewable
A decision under subsection 161(2) to require certain things of a reporting entity is reviewable
A decision under subparagraph 161(2)(d)(ii) not to allow a longer period is reviewable
A decision under subsection 191(2) to give a reporting entity a direction is reviewable

sections.source_quote: Certain decisions of the AUSTRAC CEO may be reviewed by the Administrative Review Tribunal.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reconsideration of AUSTRAC decisions by AUSTRAC CEO
sections.summary: This section details the reconsideration process for reviewable decisions made by the AUSTRAC CEO. It requires that applications for reconsideration must include specific information, be made within 30 days of being informed of the decision, and be handled by the AUSTRAC CEO personally or by a senior delegate who was not involved in the original decision. The reconsideration must be followed by written notice to the applicant.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing a process for reconsidering AUSTRAC decisions that could impact data transfer approvals, particularly for financial entities operating across borders.

requirements.text: An application for reconsideration must contain information required by the AML/CTF Rules
An application for reconsideration must be made within 30 days after being informed of the decision
The AUSTRAC CEO must reconsider the reviewable decision upon receiving an application
The AUSTRAC CEO must affirm, vary or revoke the reviewable decision
The AUSTRAC CEO must make other decisions if the reviewable decision is revoked
The AUSTRAC CEO must personally handle reconsideration or delegate to a senior person not involved in the original decision
The AUSTRAC CEO must give written notice to the applicant of the decision, effective date, reasons, and right to further review

sections.source_quote: Upon receiving an application under section 233D, the AUSTRAC CEO must reconsider the reviewable decision.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for specific entities and circumstances
sections.summary: This section provides exemptions for certain persons and circumstances. It specifies that the Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from certain provisions of the Act, including parts of the Act related to reporting entities and financial services. It also outlines exemptions for entities operating no more than 15 gaming machines that provide specific designated services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by identifying specific entities that may be exempt from data transfer requirements, particularly for gaming-related financial services.

requirements.text: The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from Part 1A of the Act
The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from Divisions 2 to 7 (other than section 39) of Part 2
The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from Divisions 3 to 6 (other than section 49) of Part 3
The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from Parts 4, 5 and 8
The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from Divisions 3 to 6 of Part 10
The Reserve Bank of Australia is exempt from section 236B

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Protection from liability for compliance with Act
sections.summary: This section provides protection from liability for actions taken in compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules. It states that actions taken in good faith to comply with requirements under the Act do not constitute a contravention, and that a defense can be established if the defendant proves they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the contravention.
sections.dtia_summary: This section matters for DTIAs as it establishes a defense mechanism for data transfers that comply with regulatory requirements, reducing liability for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Actions taken in compliance with the Act, regulations, or AML/CTF Rules do not constitute a contravention
A defense can be established if the defendant proves they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence

sections.source_quote: An action, suit or proceeding does not lie against a person in relation to anything done, or omitted to be done, in compliance, or in purported compliance, with a requirement under this Act, the regulations or the AML/CTF Rules.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Defense of taking reasonable precautions to avoid contraventions
sections.summary: This section establishes a defense for contraventions where the defendant proves they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the contravention. It applies to criminal proceedings, civil penalty proceedings, and proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. The defense requires the defendant to demonstrate they took reasonable steps to avoid the contravention.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by providing a defense mechanism for data transfers that comply with regulatory requirements, particularly for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: In criminal proceedings, the defendant must prove they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the contravention
In civil penalty proceedings, the defendant must prove they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the contravention
In proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, the defendant must prove they took reasonable precautions and exercised due diligence to avoid the contravention

sections.source_quote: In the proceedings, it is a defence if the defendant proves that the defendant took reasonable precautions, and exercised due diligence, to avoid the contravention in respect of which the proceedings were instituted.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Discharge of obligations by reporting group members
sections.summary: This section allows obligations imposed on reporting entities within a reporting group to be discharged by other group members. The lead entity is deemed to have received information from AUSTRAC regarding risk assessments. A civil penalty provision contravened by a non-lead entity is also considered contravened by the lead entity. The section specifies conditions for discharge and applies to reporting groups under the AML/CTF Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies how obligations within reporting groups can be discharged by members, relevant for DTIAs involving group entities where risk assessments and civil penalties may be shared across entities.

requirements.text: Obligations imposed on reporting entities in a reporting group may be discharged by other group members
